{
  "title_inferred": "Xixin (細辛) — Chinese Wild Ginger / Asarum: Names, Identification, Properties, and Medical Uses",
  "original_text": "【釋名】小辛（《本經》）、少辛。

頌曰：華州真細辛，根細而味極辛，故名之曰細辛。

時珍曰：小辛、少辛，皆此義也。按《山海經》云，浮戲之山多少辛。《管子》云，五沃之土，群藥生少辛，是矣。

【集解】《別錄》曰：細辛，生華陰山谷，二月、八月採根陰乾。弘景曰：今用東陽臨海者，形段乃好，而辛烈不及華陰、高麗者。用之去其頭節。

當之曰：細辛如葵赤黑，一根一葉相連。

頌曰：今處處有之，皆不及華陰者爲真，其根細而極辛。今人多以杜衡爲之，杜衡根似飯帚密鬧，細長四、五寸，微黃白色，江淮呼爲馬蹄香，不可誤用。

宗奭曰：細辛，葉如葵，赤黑色，非此則杜衡也。杜衡葉如馬蹄之下，故俗名馬蹄香。蓋根似白前，又似細辛。按沈括《夢溪筆談》云：細辛出華山，極細而直，柔韌，深紫色，味極辛，嚼之習習如椒而更甚於椒。本草云：細辛水漬令直，是以杜衡偽爲之也。東南所用細辛，皆杜衡也。杜衡黃白色，拳曲而脆，乾則作團，又謂之馬蹄。襄漢間又有一種細辛，極細而直，色黃白，乃是鬼督郵，亦非細辛也。

時珍曰：《博物志》言杜衡亂細辛，自古已然矣。沈氏所說甚詳。大抵能亂細辛者，不止杜衡，皆當以根苗色味細辨之。葉似小葵，柔莖細根，直而色紫，味極辛者，細辛也。葉似馬蹄，莖微粗，根曲而黃白色，味亦辛者，杜衡也。一莖直上，莖端生葉如傘，根似細辛，微粗直而黃白色，味辛微苦者，鬼督郵也。似鬼督郵而色黑者，及己也。葉似小桑，根似細辛，微粗長而黃色，味辛而有臊氣者，徐長卿也。葉似柳而根似細辛，粗長黃白色而味苦者，白薇也。似白薇而白直味甘者，白前也。

根

【氣味】辛，溫，無毒。普曰：神農、黃帝、雷公、桐君：辛，小溫；岐伯：無毒；李當之：小寒。權曰：苦、辛。之才曰：曾青、棗根爲之使。得當歸、芍藥、白芷、芎藭、牡丹、藁本、甘草，共療婦人；得決明、鯉魚膽、青羊肝，共療目痛。惡黃耆、野狼毒、山茱萸，忌生菜、狸肉，畏硝石、滑石，反藜蘆。

【主治】咳逆，上氣，頭痛，腦動，百節拘攣，風濕痺痛、死肌。久服明目、利九竅、輕身長年（《本經》）。溫中，下氣，破痰，利水道，開胸中滯結，除喉痹齆、鼻不聞香臭，風癇癲疾，下乳結，汗不出，血不行，安五臟，益肝膽，通精氣（《別錄》）。添膽氣、治嗽，去皮風濕癢，風眼淚下，除齒痛，血閉，婦人血瀝腰痛（甄權）。含之，去口臭（弘景）。潤肝燥，治督脈爲病，脊強而厥（好古）。治口舌生瘡，大便燥結，起目中倒睫（時珍）。

【發明】宗奭曰：治頭面風痛，不可缺此。

元素曰：細辛氣溫，味大辛，氣濃於味，陽也，升也，入足厥陰、少陰血分，爲手少陰引經之藥。香味俱細，故入少陰，與獨活相類。以獨活爲使，治少陰頭痛如神。亦止諸陽頭痛，諸風通用之。味辛而熱，溫少陰之經，散水氣以去內寒。

成無己曰：水停心下不行，則腎氣燥，宜辛以潤之。細辛之辛，以行水氣而潤燥。

杲曰：膽氣不足，細辛補之。又治邪氣自裡之表，故仲景少陰証，用麻黃附子細辛湯。

時珍曰：氣之濃者能發熱，陽中之陽也。辛溫能散，故諸風寒、風濕頭痛、痰飲、胸中滯氣、驚癇者，宜用之。口瘡、喉痺、齒諸病用之者，取其能散浮熱，亦火郁則發之之義也。辛能泄肺，故風寒咳嗽上氣者，宜用之。辛能補肝，故膽氣不足，驚癇眼目諸病，宜用之。辛能潤燥，故通少陰及耳竅，便澀者宜用之。

承曰：細辛非華陰者不得爲真。若單用末，不可過一錢。多則氣悶塞不通者死，雖死無傷。近年開平獄中嘗治此，不可不記。",
  "translation": "**[Names and Nomenclature]**
Alternate names: Xiǎo xīn [Small-pungent] (as recorded in the *Shennong Bencao Jing* [Divine Husbandman's Classic of Materia Medica]); Shǎo xīn [Lesser-pungent].

Su Song states: The true xixin from Huazhou has roots that are fine [slender] and a flavor that is extremely acrid/pungent [xīn], hence the name xìxīn [fine-pungent].

Li Shizhen states: Xiǎo xīn and shǎo xīn

results.title_inferred: Du Heng (Wild Ginger / Horse-hoof Incense): Names, Description, and Medical Uses
results.original_text:
【釋名】杜葵（《綱目》）、馬蹄香（《唐本》）、土鹵（《爾雅》）、土細辛（《綱目》）。

恭曰：杜衡，葉似葵，形似馬蹄，故俗名馬蹄香。頌曰：《爾雅》杜又名土鹵，然杜若亦名杜衡，或疑是杜若。而郭璞注云：似葵，當是杜衡也。

【集解】《別錄》曰：杜衡生山谷，三月三日采根，熟洗曝乾。弘景曰：根葉都似細辛，惟氣小異爾。處處有之。方藥少用，惟道家服之。令人身衣香。恭曰：生山之陰，水澤下濕地。葉似葵，形如馬蹄。根似細辛、白前等。今俗以及己代之，謬矣。及己獨莖，莖端四葉，葉間白花，殊無芳氣。有毒，服之令人吐，惟療瘡疥，不可亂杜衡也。頌曰：今江淮間皆有之。春初於宿根上生苗，葉似馬蹄下狀，高二、三寸，莖如麥稿粗細，每窠上有五七葉，或八九葉，別無枝蔓。又於莖葉間罅內蘆頭上貼地生紫花，其花似見不見，暗結實如豆大，窠內有碎子，似天仙子。苗葉俱青，經霜即枯，其根成空，有似飯帚密鬧，細長四五寸，粗於細辛，微黃白色，味辛，江淮俗呼爲馬蹄香。謹按《山海經》云：天帝之山有草焉。其狀如葵，其臭如蘼蕪，名曰杜衡。可以走馬，食之已癭。郭璞注云：帶之可以走馬。或曰：馬得之而健走也。宗奭曰：杜衡用根似細辛，但根色白，葉如馬蹄之下。市人往往以亂細辛，將二物相對，便見真偽。況細辛惟出華州者良。杜衡色黃，拳局而脆，乾則作團。詳細辛下。

時珍曰：按《土宿本草》云：杜細辛，葉圓如馬蹄，紫背者良，江南、荊、湖、川、陝、閩、廣俱有之。取自然汁，可伏硫、砒，製汞。

根

【氣味】辛，溫，無毒。

【主治】風寒咳逆。作浴湯，香人衣體（《別錄》）。止氣奔喘促，消痰飲，破留血、項間癭瘤之疾（甄權）。下氣殺蟲（時珍）。

【發明】時珍曰：古方吐藥往往用杜衡者，非杜衡也，乃及己也。及己似細辛而有毒，吐人。昔人多以及己當杜衡，杜衡當細辛，故爾錯誤也。杜衡則無毒，不吐人，功雖不及細辛，而亦能散風寒，下氣消痰，行水破血也。
results.translation:
**[Names]** Du Kui [lit. 'du mallow'] (Bencao Gangmu); Ma Ti Xiang ['horse-hoof fragrance'] (Tang Bencao); Tu Lu (Erya); Tu Xi Xin ['earth asarum'] (Bencao Gangmu).

Su Gong states: Du Heng has leaves resembling mallow [葵] and a shape like a horse's hoof, hence the common name 'horse-hoof fragrance.' Su Song states: The Erya records that du [杜] is also called tu lu [土鹵]. However, Du Ruo [Pollia japonica] is likewise referred to as Du Heng, so some suspect the two may be the same. Yet Guo Pu's commentary notes that it resembles mallow, which points to Du Heng being the correct identification.

**[Collected Explanations]** The Mingyi Bielu states: Du Heng grows in mountain valleys. The roots are collected on the third day of the third month [lunar calendar], washed thoroughly, and dried in the sun. Tao Hongjing states: The roots and leaves closely resemble those of Xi Xin [Asarum], differing only slightly in fragrance. It is found everywhere. It is seldom used in medical prescriptions; Daoist practitioners consume it, and it imparts a fragrant scent to the body and clothing. Su Gong states: It grows on the shaded sides of mountains, in low-lying wet areas near water and marshes. The leaves resemble mallow, shaped like a horse's hoof. The roots resemble those of Xi Xin and Bai Qian [Cynanchum]. Nowadays people commonly substitute Ji Ji [Chloranthus serratus] for it, which is a serious error. Ji Ji has a single stem with four leaves at its tip, white flowers among the leaves, and entirely lacks aromatic fragrance. It is toxic; taking it causes vomiting, and it is useful only for treating sores and scabies — it must not be confused with Du Heng. Su Song states: It is found throughout the Jianghuai region today. In early spring, new shoots arise from the old rootstock; the leaves are shaped like the underside of a horse's hoof, growing two to three cun tall. The stems are as thick as wheat straw. Each clump bears five to seven leaves, sometimes eight or nine, with no separate branches or tendrils. Purple flowers grow pressed to the ground from the nodes in the gaps between stems and leaves, at the base of the rhizome-heads; the flowers are barely visible and inconspicuous. Seeds form in concealed fashion, roughly the size of a bean, with fine seeds inside the pod resembling those of Hyoscyamus [天仙子, henbane]. Both shoots and leaves are green, withering after frost. The roots are hollow inside, densely packed like a rice-straw brush, slender and four to five cun long, coarser than Xi Xin, slightly yellowish-white in color, and pungent in flavor. In the Jianghuai region it is commonly called 'Ma Ti Xiang.' I note that the Shanhaijing records: On the Mountain of the Heavenly Emperor there is an herb shaped like mallow, with a fragrance like Mi Wu [Ligusticum striatum], named Du Heng. It can make horses run swiftly; eating it cures goiter [癭]. Guo Pu's commentary states: Carrying it on one's person can make horses run swiftly; or, horses that eat it become vigorous runners. Kou Zongshi states: The root of Du Heng, used medicinally, resembles Xi Xin, but is white in color, and the leaves resemble the underside of a horse's hoof. Merchants often use it to adulterate Xi Xin; placing the two side by side will reveal which is genuine. Moreover, only Xi Xin produced in Huazhou is of superior quality. Du Heng is yellowish, coiled and brittle, and forms a tight clump when dried. See also the entry on Xi Xin for further details.

Li Shizhen states: According to the Tushu Bencao ['Earthly Lodging Materia Medica']: Du Xi Xin has round leaves like a horse's hoof; specimens with purple undersides are superior. It is found throughout Jiangnan, Jing, Hu, Chuan, Shaanxi, Min, and Guang. The raw juice can be used to immobilize [伏, render inert/fix] sulfur and arsenic, and to process mercury [in alchemical preparations].

**Root**

**[Qi and Flavor]** Pungent; warm; non-toxic.

**[Indications and Treatments]**
- Wind-cold cough with rebellious qi [wind-cold咳逆]. Prepared as a bath decoction, it imparts fragrance to the body and clothing. (Mingyi Bielu)
- Stops rushing qi [奔喘] and labored breathing; disperses phlegm-fluid accumulation [痰飲]; breaks up static blood; treats goiter and tumor growths at the neck [項間癭瘤]. (Zhen Quan)
- Descends qi; kills parasites/worms. (Li Shizhen)

**[Elucidation]** Li Shizhen states: In ancient prescriptions, emetic formulas that list Du Heng as an ingredient do not actually refer to Du Heng — they mean Ji Ji [Chloranthus serratus]. Ji Ji resembles Xi Xin but is toxic and induces vomiting. In earlier times, people often mistook Ji Ji for Du Heng, and Du Heng for Xi Xin, hence the accumulated errors. Du Heng itself is non-toxic and does not cause vomiting. Though its efficacy does not match that of Xi Xin, it is nonetheless capable of dispersing wind-cold, descending qi, dispersing phlegm, promoting water movement, and breaking up static blood.

entities_mentioned.name: 杜衡 (Du Heng)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Asarum forbesii Maxim. (Forbes' wild ginger)
entities_mentioned.notes: A member of the Aristolochiaceae family, closely related to Xi Xin (Asarum sieboldii). Distinguished from Xi Xin by its whitish, coarser roots and horse-hoof-shaped leaves.

entities_mentioned.name: 細辛 (Xi Xin)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Asarum sieboldii Miq. or Asarum heterotropoides Fr. Schmidt
entities_mentioned.notes: Frequently confused with Du Heng in the marketplace. Text notes Huazhou-produced Xi Xin is considered superior.

entities_mentioned.name: 及己 (Ji Ji)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Chloranthus serratus (Thunb.) Roem. & Schult.
entities_mentioned.notes: Toxic plant sometimes fraudulently substituted for Du Heng. Causes vomiting; used only externally for sores and scabies. Has a single stem, four apical leaves, and white flowers.

entities_mentioned.name: 白前 (Bai Qian)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cynanchum glaucescens (Decne.) Hand.-Mazz. or Cynanchum stauntonii (Decne.) Schltr.
entities_mentioned.notes: Mentioned only for morphological comparison of root form.

entities_mentioned.name: 杜若 (Du Ruo)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Pollia japonica Thunb.
entities_mentioned.notes: Mentioned as a potential source of confusion with Du Heng in classical texts; dismissed by Guo Pu's commentary.

entities_mentioned.name: 蘼蕪 (Mi Wu)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ligusticum striatum DC. (Chinese lovage) or Ligusticum chuanxiong Hort.
entities_mentioned.notes: Cited from the Shanhaijing as a fragrance reference for Du Heng.

entities_mentioned.name: 天仙子 (Tian Xian Zi)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Hyoscyamus niger L. (black henbane)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a morphological reference for the seed size and appearance of Du Heng.

results.translator_notes: 1. The term 伏硫、砒，製汞 refers to alchemical/proto-chemical operations of 'fixing' or neutralizing sulfur and arsenic and processing/refining mercury — a Daoist alchemical usage not a medical one. 2. 癭 (ying) is rendered as 'goiter' throughout, though it could refer to any neck swelling or tumor; the text explicitly mentions cervical goiter/tumor (項間癭瘤). 3. The Tushu Bencao (土宿本草) cited by Li Shizhen is an obscure alchemical/materia medica text; its authorship is uncertain. 4. 奔喘 (ben chuan) is rendered as 'rushing qi and labored breathing,' reflecting a TCM concept of qi moving rebelliously upward causing dyspnea. 5. Li Shizhen's 'Elucidation' section is particularly important as it corrects a longstanding historical misidentification: ancient emetic formulas attributed to Du Heng actually used the toxic Ji Ji.

results.title_inferred: Jiji (及己) — '獐耳細辛' (Deer-ear Asarum), a toxic herb used for skin ailments and sores
results.original_text:
【釋名】獐耳細辛。時珍曰：及己名義未詳。二月生苗，先開白花，後方生葉三片，狀如獐耳，根如細辛，故名獐耳細辛。

【集解】恭曰：及己生山谷陰虛軟地。其草一莖，莖頭四葉，隙著白花。根似細辛而黑，有

根

【氣味】苦，平，有毒。恭曰：入口使人吐血。

【主治】諸惡瘡疥痂蝕，及牛馬諸瘡（《唐本》）。頭瘡白禿風瘙，皮膚蟲癢，可煎汁浸並敷之（大明）。殺蟲（時珍）。

【發明】弘景曰：今人以合瘡疥膏，甚驗。時珍曰：今人不知及己，往往以當杜衡，卻以杜衡當細辛，故杜衡諸方多是及己也。辯見細辛、杜衡二條。
results.translation:
**Explanatory Names:** [Also called] 'Deer-ear Asarum' [獐耳細辛]. Li Shizhen states: The meaning of the name 及己 (Jiji) is not entirely clear. In the second month [of the lunar calendar] the seedlings emerge; white flowers bloom first, and only afterward three leaves appear, shaped like the ears of a river deer [獐, Hydropotes inermis]. The roots resemble those of Asarum [細辛], hence the name 'Deer-ear Asarum.'

**Collected Explanations:** Su Gong states: Jiji grows in the shaded, moist, soft soils of mountain valleys. The plant has a single stem with four leaves at the top, and white flowers appear in the interstices [between the leaves]. The roots resemble those of Asarum but are black [the remainder of this passage appears incomplete or truncated in the source].

**Flavor and Nature:** Bitter; balanced [平]; toxic. Su Gong states: If taken by mouth, it causes a person to vomit blood.

**Indications and Uses:** [Treats] all manner of malignant sores, scabies [疥], crusted and erosive lesions, as well as various sores on cattle and horses (cited from the *Tang Materia Medica* [《唐本草》]). [For] head sores, bald white ringworm [白禿], wind-induced itching, and skin parasites causing itchiness — decoct the juice and use it to soak [the affected area] or apply it topically (cited from Daming [《大明本草》]). Kills parasites/insects (Li Shizhen).

**Elucidation:** Tao Hongjing states: People today use it compounded into an ointment [膏] for sores and scabies, with very good results. Li Shizhen states: People today do not recognise Jiji and frequently mistake it for Duheng [杜衡, *Asarum forbesii*], while also mistaking Duheng for Asarum [細辛]; consequently, prescriptions calling for Duheng are in many cases actually Jiji. See the entries on Asarum and Duheng for further discussion.

entities_mentioned.name: 及己 (Jiji)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Chloranthus serratus (Thunb.) Roem. & Schult. (family Chloranthaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: A toxic herb with a single stem, four leaves at the apex, white flowers, and black roots resembling Asarum. The morphological description — single stem, four leaves, white interstitial flowers, black Asarum-like roots — is consistent with Chloranthus serratus, a well-established identification in modern Chinese botanical scholarship.

entities_mentioned.name: 獐耳細辛 (Deer-ear Asarum)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Chloranthus serratus (Thunb.) Roem. & Schult.
entities_mentioned.notes: Vernacular synonym for 及己; the name references the deer-ear-shaped leaves and Asarum-like roots.

entities_mentioned.name: 細辛 (Asarum)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Asarum species (e.g., Asarum sieboldii Miq. or related spp.)
entities_mentioned.notes: Referenced here as a morphological comparator for the roots of 及己. Also mentioned as a drug commonly confused with 及己 and 杜衡 in practice.

entities_mentioned.name: 杜衡 (Duheng)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Asarum forbesii Maxim.
entities_mentioned.notes: Li Shizhen warns that 及己 was frequently misidentified as 杜衡, and 杜衡 as 細辛, creating a chain of substitution errors in clinical use.

results.translator_notes: 1. The 【集解】section is clearly truncated in the source text; after 'the roots resemble those of Asarum but are black, have…' the passage breaks off with only the word 根 (root) on a separate line, suggesting OCR loss or a lacuna in the source. This has been flagged in the translation. 2. Su Gong (蘇恭) was the lead compiler of the *Xinxiu Bencao* (新修本草, 659 CE), here abbreviated 《唐本》. 3. 'Daming' (大明) refers to Ri Huazi (日華子), author of the *Rihuazi Bencao* (日華子本草, c. 10th century). 4. The preparation method described — decocting the juice for soaking or topical application — is external use only, consistent with the plant's noted oral toxicity.

results.title_inferred: Gui Du You (Ghost Postal Supervisor Herb) — Root Entry
results.original_text:
【釋名】獨搖草（《唐本》）。時珍曰：此草獨莖而葉攢其端，無風自動，故曰鬼獨搖草，後人訛爲鬼督郵爾。因其專主鬼病，猶司鬼之督郵也。古者傳舍有督郵之官主之。徐長卿、赤箭皆治鬼病，故並有鬼督郵之名，名同而物異。

【集解】恭曰：鬼督郵所在有之。有必叢生，苗惟一莖，莖端生葉若傘狀，根如牛膝而細黑。今人以徐長卿代之，非也。保升曰：莖似細箭杆，高二尺以下。葉生莖端，狀如傘。花生葉心，黃白色。根橫生而無須，二月、八月採根。徐長卿、赤箭並有鬼督郵之名，而主治不同，宜審用之。時珍曰：鬼督郵與及己同類，根苗皆相似。但以根如細辛而色黑者，爲及己；根如細辛而色黃白者，爲鬼督郵。

根

【氣味】辛、苦，平，無毒。時珍曰：有小毒。

【主治】鬼疰卒忤中惡，心腹邪氣，百精毒，溫瘧疫疾，強腰腳，益膂力（《唐本》）。

【發明】時珍曰：按東晉《深師方》，治上氣嗽、飲嗽、邪嗽、燥嗽、冷嗽，四滿丸，用鬼督郵同蜈蚣、芫花、躑躅諸毒藥爲丸，則其有毒可知矣。非毒藥不能治鬼疰邪惡之病，《唐本》云無毒，蓋不然。
results.translation:
**[Names and Nomenclature]**
Alternate name: Dú Yáo Cǎo ["Solitary-Swaying Herb"] (from the *Newly Revised Materia Medica* [Tang Bencao]). Li Shizhen states: This plant has a single stem with leaves clustered at its tip, and it sways of its own accord even without wind. Hence it was called Guǐ Dú Yáo Cǎo ["Ghost's Solitary-Swaying Herb"]; later people corrupted this into Guǐ Dū Yóu ["Ghost Postal Supervisor"]. Because it specializes in treating ghost-affliction diseases [guǐ bìng], it is likened to a postal supervisor [dū yóu] overseeing ghosts. In ancient times, the postal relay stations [chuán shè] were administered by officials called dū yóu. Both Xú Cháng Qīng [Cynanchum paniculatum] and Chì Jiàn [Gastrodia elata, "Red Arrow"] treat ghost-affliction diseases and thus share the name Guǐ Dū Yóu—same name, different plants.

**[Collected Explanations]**
Sū Gōng states: Guǐ Dū Yóu grows in many places. Where it grows, it invariably forms clumps; the plant has only a single stem, with leaves clustered at the tip in an umbrella-like form, and roots resembling those of Niú Xī [Achyranthes bidentata] but finer and black in color. Nowadays people substitute Xú Cháng Qīng for it, which is incorrect. Hán Bǎoshēng states: The stem resembles a slender arrow shaft and grows no taller than two Chinese feet [approximately 60 cm]. The leaves grow at the tip of the stem in an umbrella shape. The flowers emerge from the center of the leaves and are yellow-white in color. The roots grow horizontally and have no rootlets; the roots are collected in the second and eighth months [of the lunar calendar]. Both Xú Cháng Qīng and Chì Jiàn share the name Guǐ Dū Yóu, yet their therapeutic applications differ, so one must use them with careful discernment. Li Shizhen states: Guǐ Dū Yóu belongs to the same category as Jǐ Jǐ [Chloranthus serratus], and their roots and shoots closely resemble one another. The distinction is: roots resembling Xì Xīn [Asarum] in form but black in color are Jǐ Jǐ; roots resembling Xì Xīn but yellow-white in color are Guǐ Dū Yóu.

**Root**

**[Qi and Flavor]**
Acrid and bitter; balanced [píng]; non-toxic. Li Shizhen states: [It is in fact] mildly toxic.

**[Indications and Treatments]**
Ghost-affliction and sudden-onset malign possession [guǐ zhù cù wǔ zhōng è]; evil qi [邪氣] in the chest and abdomen; the toxins of the hundred spirits [bǎi jīng dú]; warm malaria and epidemic diseases; strengthening the lumbar region and legs; and building muscular strength in the back. (From the *Tang Bencao*.)

**[Discussion and Elaboration]**
Li Shizhen states: According to the Eastern Jin dynasty *Fang of Master Shen* [Shēn Shī Fāng], the "Four Fullnesses Pill" [Sì Mǎn Wán], used to treat ascending-qi cough, phlegm-fluid cough, evil-qi cough, dry cough, and cold cough, combines Guǐ Dū Yóu with centipede [wú gōng], genkwa [yuán huā], azalea [zhí zhú], and other toxic drugs to form pills—from which it is clear that this herb is indeed toxic. Only toxic drugs are capable of treating ghost-affliction and malign evil diseases. The *Tang Bencao*'s claim that it is non-toxic is simply mistaken.

entities_mentioned.name: 鬼督郵 (Guǐ Dū Yóu)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Chloranthus japonicus or a related Chloranthus sp.; some sources tentatively identify it with Aristolochia or Asarum-allied plants, but most likely a Chloranthus species based on morphological description
entities_mentioned.notes: The umbrella-shaped leaf arrangement at the tip of a single stem and horizontally growing rootless rhizome are consistent with certain Chloranthus species. Li Shizhen himself notes its close resemblance to 及己 (Chloranthus serratus).

entities_mentioned.name: 獨搖草 (Dú Yáo Cǎo)
entities_mentioned.notes: Alternate name for 鬼督郵; not a separate plant.

entities_mentioned.name: 徐長卿 (Xú Cháng Qīng)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cynanchum paniculatum (Bunge) Kitag.
entities_mentioned.notes: A distinct plant that shares the folk name Guǐ Dū Yóu; Li Shizhen warns against confusing it with the true Guǐ Dū Yóu.

entities_mentioned.name: 赤箭 (Chì Jiàn)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Gastrodia elata Blume
entities_mentioned.notes: Another plant sharing the name Guǐ Dū Yóu in folk usage; the rhizome (天麻, Tiān Má) is the part used medicinally.

entities_mentioned.name: 及己 (Jǐ Jǐ)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Chloranthus serratus (Thunb.) Roem. & Schult.
entities_mentioned.notes: Distinguished from Guǐ Dū Yóu by root color: Jǐ Jǐ has black roots, Guǐ Dū Yóu has yellow-white roots.

entities_mentioned.name: 牛膝 (Niú Xī)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Achyranthes bidentata Blume
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a morphological reference for the root of Guǐ Dū Yóu.

entities_mentioned.name: 細辛 (Xì Xīn)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Asarum heterotropoides Fr. Schmidt var. mandshuricum (Maxim.) Kitag. or related Asarum spp.
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a morphological reference for root shape.

entities_mentioned.name: 蜈蚣 (Wú Gōng)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Scolopendra subspinipes mutilans L. Koch (centipede)
entities_mentioned.notes: Ingredient in the Four Fullnesses Pill formula.

entities_mentioned.name: 芫花 (Yuán Huā)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Daphne genkwa Sieb. & Zucc.
entities_mentioned.notes: Toxic ingredient in the Four Fullnesses Pill formula.

entities_mentioned.name: 躑躅 (Zhí Zhú)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Rhododendron molle G. Don (Chinese azalea / yellow azalea)
entities_mentioned.notes: Toxic ingredient in the Four Fullnesses Pill formula; specifically refers to 羊躑躅.

results.translator_notes: 1. 'Ghost-affliction diseases' (鬼病, guǐ bìng) and related terms (鬼疰, 卒忤, 中惡) are traditional diagnostic categories describing sudden, severe, or unexplained illness attributed to malign supernatural influences; they broadly correspond to acute convulsive, febrile, or psychiatric episodes in modern terms. 2. The official title 督郵 (dū yóu) was a Han-dynasty supervisory postal/administrative official; the etymological wordplay on 'overseeing ghosts' is a folk-medical conceit. 3. Li Shizhen explicitly disputes the *Tang Bencao*'s toxicity classification, asserting mild toxicity based on the herb's co-formulation with known toxic substances. 4. The *Shēn Shī Fāng* (深師方) is attributed to a Buddhist monk-physician of the Eastern Jin period; this text does not survive independently and is known only through citations. 5. 'Two Chinese feet' (二尺) in the Tang/Song system ≈ approximately 60–66 cm.

results.original_text:
【釋名】鬼督郵（《本經》）、別仙蹤（蘇頌）。

時珍曰：徐長卿，人名也，常以此藥治邪病，人遂以名之。《名醫別錄》於有名未用復出石下長卿條，云一名徐長卿。陶弘景注云：此是誤爾。方家無用，亦不復識。今考二條功療相似。按《吳普本草》云：徐長卿一名石下長卿。其爲一物甚明，但石間生者爲良。前人欠氣亦相似。今狗脊散用鬼督郵者，取其強悍宜腰腳，故知是徐長卿，而非鬼箭、赤箭。

【集解】《別錄》曰：徐長卿，生泰山山谷及隴西，三月採。又曰：石下長卿生隴西山谷池澤，三月採。恭曰：所在川澤有之。葉似柳，兩葉相當，有光澤。根如細辛，微粗長，黃色而有臊氣。今俗以代鬼督郵，非也。鬼督郵自有本條。保升曰：生下濕川澤之間。苗似小桑，兩葉相對。三月苗青，七月、八月著子，似蘿摩子而小。九月苗黃，十月凋。八月採根，晒乾。頌曰：今淄、齊、淮、泗間皆有之，三月、四月採，謂之別仙蹤。時珍曰：鬼督郵、及己之亂杜衡，其功不同，苗亦不同也。徐長卿之亂鬼督郵，其苗不同，其功同也。杜衡之亂細辛，則根苗功用皆仿佛，乃彌近而大亂也。不可不審。

根

【氣味】辛，溫，無毒。《別錄》曰：石下長卿：鹹，平，有毒。普曰：徐長卿一名石下長卿。神農、雷公：辛。時珍曰：治鬼之藥多有毒，當從《別錄》。

【主治】鬼物百精蠱毒，疫疾邪惡氣，溫瘧。久服強悍輕身（《本經》）。益氣延年。又曰：石下長卿：主鬼疰精物邪惡氣，殺百精蠱毒，老魅注易，亡走啼哭，悲傷恍惚（《別錄》）。

【發明】時珍曰：《抱朴子》言：上古辟瘟疫有徐長卿散，良效。今人不知用此。
results.translation:
**[Explanation of Names]** Also known as Guidupou [Ghost Superintendent's Mail] (Shennong Bencao Jing), and Bié Xiān Zōng [Traces of a Parting Immortal] (Su Song).

Li Shizhen states: Xu Changqing was a person's name. He regularly used this herb to treat illnesses caused by evil [spirits], and people thereafter named the plant after him. The Mingyi Bielu [Supplementary Records of Famous Physicians], in its section of named but unused drugs, lists a separate entry for Shixia Changqing [Stone-dwelling Changqing], stating that one of its alternate names is Xu Changqing. Tao Hongjing's annotation remarks: this is simply an error, as no physician uses it and it is no longer recognisable. On examining both entries today, their therapeutic properties are alike. Wu Pu's Bencao [Materia Medica] states: Xu Changqing is also called Shixia Changqing. That they are one and the same plant is quite clear; it is merely that the variety growing among rocks is considered superior. Earlier authorities' [assessments of] qi are also similar. The Gouji San [Cibotium Powder] that employs Guidupou does so for its vigorous, bracing effect on the lower back and legs — this confirms that it is Xu Changqing, and not Guijian [Euonymus alatus] or Chijian [Gastrodia elata].

**[Collected Explanations]** The Bielu states: Xu Changqing grows in mountain valleys of Taishan and Longxi; it is gathered in the third month. It further states: Shixia Changqing grows in mountain valleys, pools, and marshes of Longxi; it is gathered in the third month. [Su] Gong states: it is found wherever there are rivers and marshes. The leaves resemble those of the willow, growing in opposite pairs, and are glossy. The root resembles that of Asarum [Xixin], but is slightly thicker and longer, yellow in colour, and has a rank [goat-like] odour. It is now popularly used as a substitute for Guidupou, which is incorrect — Guidupou has its own proper entry. [Han] Baosheng states: it grows in low-lying, damp riverside marshes. The shoots resemble those of a small mulberry, with two leaves growing opposite each other. In the third month the shoots are green; in the seventh and eighth months it bears seeds resembling those of Metaplexis japonica [Luomo] but smaller. In the ninth month the shoots yellow; by the tenth month the plant withers. The root is gathered in the eighth month and dried in the sun. [Su] Song states: it is now found throughout the regions of Zi, Qi, Huai, and Si; it is collected in the third and fourth months and is called Bié Xiān Zōng [Traces of a Parting Immortal]. Li Shizhen states: The confusion of Guidupou and Jiji [Chloranthus serratus] with Duheng [Asarum forbesii] involves plants that differ in both appearance and therapeutic function. The confusion of Xu Changqing with Guidupou involves plants whose shoots differ, yet whose therapeutic functions are the same. The confusion of Duheng with Xixin [Asarum], however, involves plants whose roots, shoots, and functions are all alike — this is a far closer and therefore far more serious confusion, and one must examine it with great care.

**Root**

**[Qi and Flavour]** Acrid [xin], warm, non-toxic. The Bielu states: Shixia Changqing — salty, neutral [ping], toxic. [Wu] Pu states: Xu Changqing, also named Shixia Changqing — Shennong and Leigong both classify it as acrid. Li Shizhen states: drugs used to treat ghost[-related) ailments are for the most part toxic; one should follow the Bielu's classification.

**[Principal Indications]** Demonic entities, the hundred essences [bai jing], and gu-poison [parasitic toxin]; epidemic pestilence and evil malignant qi; warm malaria [wen nüe]. Long-term use fortifies and invigorates the body and makes it light (Shennong Bencao Jing). It also augments qi and prolongs life. Regarding Shixia Changqing: it governs ghost-possession [gui zhu], the qi of malignant spirit-essences, and kills the hundred essences and gu-poison; [it treats] afflictions transmitted by old demons, wandering about and weeping, grief, and mental confusion (Mingyi Bielu).

**[Elucidation]** Li Shizhen states: The Baopuzi records that in high antiquity there was a powder called Xu Changqing San used to ward off epidemic pestilence, with excellent efficacy. People today are unaware of this use.

entities_mentioned.name: 徐長卿 (Xu Changqing)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cynanchum paniculatum (Bunge) Kitag. (family Apocynaceae/Asclepiadaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Also historically listed as Shixia Changqing (石下長卿); Li Shizhen argues these are the same plant. Root is the primary medicinal part. Also known by the alternate names Guidupou and Bié Xiān Zōng.

entities_mentioned.name: 鬼督郵 (Guidupou)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Aristolochia or Pyrola species; historically conflated with Xu Changqing
entities_mentioned.notes: Li Shizhen distinguishes Guidupou from Guijian (Euonymus alatus) and Chijian (Gastrodia elata), and argues it is functionally equivalent to Xu Changqing despite morphological differences.

entities_mentioned.name: 細辛 (Xixin)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Asarum heterotropoides or related Asarum species
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as morphological comparator for the root of Xu Changqing.

entities_mentioned.name: 杜衡 (Duheng)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Asarum forbesii Maxim.
entities_mentioned.notes: Mentioned as a species frequently confused with both Guidupou/Jiji and Xixin.

entities_mentioned.name: 及己 (Jiji)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Chloranthus serratus (Thunb.) Roem. & Schult.
entities_mentioned.notes: Mentioned as a plant sometimes confused with Duheng.

entities_mentioned.name: 鬼箭 (Guijian)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Euonymus alatus (Thunb.) Sieb.
entities_mentioned.notes: Distinguished from Xu Changqing by Li Shizhen.

entities_mentioned.name: 赤箭 (Chijian)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Gastrodia elata Blume
entities_mentioned.notes: Distinguished from Xu Changqing by Li Shizhen.

entities_mentioned.name: 蘿摩 (Luomo)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Metaplexis japonica (Thunb.) Makino
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a morphological comparator for the seeds/fruits of Xu Changqing.

results.translator_notes:
1. The phrase '前人欠氣亦相似' is likely corrupt or garbled (possibly an OCR/transcription error); it has been rendered tentatively as '[Earlier authorities' assessments of] qi are also similar,' but the original may have read differently (e.g., 欠 may be a corruption of another character). Flagged as potentially problematic.
2. 'Gu-poison' (蠱毒) refers to a traditional Chinese medical concept of parasitic or toxic afflictions sometimes attributed to sorcery; it is retained in translation with a bracketed gloss.
3. Conditions described (e.g., ghost-possession, malignant spirit qi) are traditional Chinese medical categories; they are translated literally to preserve the period terminology.
4. The Baopuzi (抱朴子), cited in the Elucidation section, is a 4th-century CE Daoist text by Ge Hong — its citation here lends classical authority to the drug's use in epidemic prevention.
5. The drug preparation method described is: root gathered in the eighth month, sun-dried. The Xu Changqing San (powder) mentioned in the Elucidation is referenced but not described in detail in this segment.

results.title_inferred: Baiwei (Cynanchum atratum) — Root monograph from Bencao Gangmu, Chapter Cao zhi Er
results.original_text:
【釋名】薇草（《別錄》）、白幕（《別錄》）、春草（《別錄》）、䕵（音尾）、骨美。時珍曰：薇，細也。其根細而白也。按：《爾雅》：䕵，春草也。薇、䕵音相近，則白薇又䕵音之轉也。《別錄》以䕵爲莽草之名，誤矣。

【集解】《別錄》曰：白薇生平原川谷。三月三日采根陰乾。弘景曰：近道處處有之。

頌曰：今陝西諸郡及舒、滁、潤、遼州亦有之。莖葉俱青，頗類柳葉。六、七月開紅花，八月結實。其根黃白色，類牛膝而短小，今人八月採之。

根

【氣味】苦、鹹，平，無毒。《別錄》曰：大寒。之才曰：惡黃芪、大黃、大戟、乾薑、大棗、干漆、山茱萸。

【主治】暴中風身熱肢滿，忽忽不知人，狂惑邪氣，寒熱酸疼，溫瘧洗洗，發作有時（《本經》）。療傷中淋露，下水氣，利陰氣，益精。久服利人（《別錄》）。治驚邪風狂癇病，百邪鬼魅（弘景）。風溫灼熱多眠，及熱淋遺尿，金瘡出血（時珍）。

【發明】好古曰：古方多用治婦人，以本草有療傷中淋露之故也。

時珍曰：白薇古人多用，後世罕能知之。按：張仲景治婦人產中虛煩嘔逆，安中益氣，竹皮丸方中，用白薇同桂枝各一分，竹皮、石膏各三分，甘草七分。棗肉爲大丸，每以飲化一丸服。云有熱者，倍白薇，則白薇性寒，乃陽明經藥也。徐之才《藥對》言白薇惡大棗，而此方又以棗肉爲丸，蓋恐諸藥寒涼傷脾胃爾。朱肱《活人書》治風溫發汗後，身猶灼熱，自汗身重多眠，鼻息必鼾，語言難出者，葳蕤湯中亦用之。孫真人《千金方》，有詔書發汗白薇散焉。
results.translation:
**[Names and Appellations]**
Alternate names: Weicao [Slender Herb] (Bielu), Baimu [White Veil] (Bielu), Chuncao [Spring Herb] (Bielu), 䕵 (pronounced 'wei'), and Gumei. Li Shizhen states: 'Wei [薇] means slender/fine. Its roots are fine and white.' According to the Erya: '䕵 is the spring herb.' The pronunciations of wei [薇] and 䕵 are close, so baiwei [白薇] is a phonetic transformation of 䕵. The Bielu's identification of 䕵 as a name for mangcao [Illicium lanceolatum, a toxic plant] is erroneous.

**[Collected Explanations]**
The Bielu states: Baiwei grows in flatland valleys and ravines. The root is collected on the third day of the third month and dried in the shade. Tao Hongjing states: It is found everywhere along the roadsides near inhabited areas.

Su Song states: Today it is found in various commanderies of Shaanxi, and also in Shu, Chu, Run, and Liaozhou. The stems and leaves are both green, resembling willow leaves. It opens red flowers in the sixth and seventh months, and bears fruit in the eighth month. Its roots are yellow-white in color, resembling those of niuxi [Achyranthes bidentata] but shorter and smaller; people today collect it in the eighth month.

**Root**

**[Qi and Flavor]**
Bitter and salty; balanced [ping]; non-toxic. The Bielu states: greatly cold [da han]. Xu Zhicai states: It is incompatible with [e] huangqi [Astragalus], dahuang [rhubarb], daji [Euphorbia], dried ginger, Chinese dates [dazao], dried lacquer [ganqi], and Asiatic cornelian cherry [shanzhuyu].

**[Principal Indications]**
Sudden wind-stroke [apoplexy] with bodily fever and fullness of the limbs, sudden loss of consciousness of others, manic confusion due to pathogenic qi, alternating cold and heat with aching pain, warm malaria [wenüe] with shivering, occurring at fixed intervals (Shennong Bencao Jing). Treats internal damage with dribbling discharge [linlu], disperses water qi, benefits yin qi, and augments essence. Prolonged use is beneficial to the person (Bielu). Treats fright-induced pathogenic wind-mania and epileptic disorders [jingxie feng kuang xian bing], and all manner of demonic and spectral afflictions (Hongjing). Wind-warmth [fengwen] with scorching fever and excessive somnolence, as well as hot strangury [re lin], urinary incontinence, and bleeding from metal-inflicted wounds (Shizhen).

**[Elucidations]**
Wang Haogu states: Ancient formulas frequently employed it to treat women, because the materia medica records its efficacy in treating internal damage with dribbling discharge.

Li Shizhen states: Baiwei was widely used by the ancients but is rarely understood by later generations. According to Zhang Zhongjing's formula for treating postpartum deficiency-vexation and counterflow vomiting in women — the Zhupi Wan [Bamboo Bark Pill] for calming the center and augmenting qi — baiwei and guizhi [Cinnamomum cassia twig] are each used at one fen, bamboo bark [zhupi] and gypsum [shigao] each at three fen, and licorice [gancao] at seven fen. Large pills are made with jujube flesh, and one pill is dissolved in liquid and taken per dose. It is said that in cases with heat, the dose of baiwei is doubled — indicating that baiwei is cold in nature and is a medicinal of the Yangming channel. Xu Zhicai's Yaodui states that baiwei is incompatible with Chinese dates, yet this formula uses jujube flesh to form the pills — this is evidently out of concern that the cold and cooling properties of the other drugs might damage the spleen and stomach. Zhu Hong's Huoren Shu [Book for Reviving the Living], in treating the condition after sweating induced for wind-warmth where the body remains scorching hot, with spontaneous sweating, heaviness of the body, excessive somnolence, nasal snoring, and difficulty producing speech, also uses baiwei in the Weirui Tang [Solomon's Seal Decoction]. The Immortal Master Sun's Qianjin Fang [Formulas Worth a Thousand Gold] contains an imperially proclaimed sweating formula called Baiwei San [Baiwei Powder].

entities_mentioned.name: 白薇 (Baiwei)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cynanchum atratum Bunge or Cynanchum versicolor Bunge (Asclepiadaceae); roots used medicinally
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as having white, fine roots; stems and leaves resembling willow; red flowers in months 6–7; fruit in month 8. The root is the medicinal part. Indicated for fever, altered consciousness, malaria, strangury, postpartum vomiting, and wind-warmth.

entities_mentioned.name: 莽草 (Mangcao)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Illicium lanceolatum (toxic anise species)
entities_mentioned.notes: Mentioned only to refute the Bielu's erroneous identification of the character 䕵 as a name for mangcao.

entities_mentioned.name: 牛膝 (Niuxi)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Achyranthes bidentata Blume
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a morphological comparison for the root of baiwei — similar in appearance but shorter and smaller.

entities_mentioned.name: 黃芪 (Huangqi)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Astragalus membranaceus (Fisch.) Bunge
entities_mentioned.notes: Listed as an incompatible drug (e) with baiwei.

entities_mentioned.name: 大黃 (Dahuang)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Rheum palmatum L. or related Rheum spp.
entities_mentioned.notes: Listed as incompatible with baiwei.

entities_mentioned.name: 大戟 (Daji)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Euphorbia pekinensis Rupr.
entities_mentioned.notes: Listed as incompatible with baiwei.

entities_mentioned.name: 山茱萸 (Shanzhuyu)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cornus officinalis Siebold & Zucc.
entities_mentioned.notes: Listed as incompatible with baiwei.

entities_mentioned.name: 桂枝 (Guizhi)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cinnamomum cassia Presl (twig)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used alongside baiwei in Zhang Zhongjing's Zhupi Wan at equal proportion (one fen each).

entities_mentioned.name: 竹皮 (Zhupi)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Bambusa or Phyllostachys spp. — inner bamboo shavings
entities_mentioned.notes: Component of Zhupi Wan at three fen.

entities_mentioned.name: 石膏 (Shigao)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Gypsum (calcium sulfate, CaSO4·2H2O)
entities_mentioned.notes: Component of Zhupi Wan at three fen.

entities_mentioned.name: 甘草 (Gancao)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Glycyrrhiza uralensis Fisch.
entities_mentioned.notes: Component of Zhupi Wan at seven fen.

entities_mentioned.name: 大棗 (Dazao)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ziziphus jujuba Mill.
entities_mentioned.notes: Technically listed as incompatible with baiwei (per Xu Zhicai), yet used as the binding medium (jujube flesh) for Zhupi Wan pills — explained as a measure to protect the spleen and stomach from the formula's coldness.

entities_mentioned.name: 葳蕤湯 (Weirui Tang)
entities_mentioned.notes: Formula from Zhu Hong's Huoren Shu containing baiwei; used for wind-warmth with post-sweating residual fever, spontaneous sweating, somnolence, and dysphasia. Weirui (葳蕤) refers to Polygonatum odoratum / Solomon's Seal.

entities_mentioned.name: 白薇散 (Baiwei San)
entities_mentioned.notes: An imperially decreed diaphoretic formula recorded in Sun Simiao's Qianjin Fang, containing baiwei.

results.translator_notes:
1. The character 䕵 (OCR rendered inconsistently in the source as a blank or box) is reconstructed from context as the archaic grass radical character pronounced 'wei' (tail tone), as confirmed by the phonological discussion in the text.
2. 'E' (惡) in pharmaceutical contexts means 'is antagonistic to' or 'is incompatible with' — a classical drug-interaction category; rendered as 'incompatible with' throughout.
3. The Yangming channel reference locates baiwei within the classical meridian/channel pharmacology system of Chinese medicine (Yangming = stomach/large intestine channels, associated with febrile conditions).
4. 'Linlu' (淋露) is a gynecological term referring to dribbling or spotting discharge, likely covering abnormal uterine bleeding and/or leukorrhea.
5. The dosage unit 'fen' (分) here is a classical weight unit (approximately 0.3–0.4 g in Song–Ming context) used in proportional formulation.
6. Plant identification for baiwei as Cynanchum atratum is well-established in modern Chinese pharmacopoeia (Chinese Pharmacopoeia 2020, Part I).

results.title_inferred: Baiqian (White前) — Root, Flavour, and Medical Uses
results.original_text:
【釋名】石藍（《唐本》）、嗽藥（同上）。時珍曰：名義未詳。

【集解】弘景曰：白前出近道，根似細辛而大，色白不柔易折，氣嗽方多用之。恭曰：苗高尺許，其葉似柳，或似芫花，根長於細辛，白色。生州渚沙磧之上，不生近道。俗名石藍，又名嗽藥。今用蔓生者味苦，非真也。志曰：根似白薇、牛膝輩，二月、八月採，陰乾用。

嘉謨曰：似牛膝，粗長堅直易斷者，白前也。似牛膝，短小柔軟能彎者，白薇也。近道俱有，形色頗同，以此別之，不致差誤。

根

【氣味】甘，微溫，無毒。權曰：辛。恭曰：微寒。

【主治】胸脅逆氣，咳嗽上氣，呼吸欲絕（《別錄》）。主一切氣，肺氣煩悶，奔豚腎氣（大明）。

【發明】宗奭曰：白前能保定肺氣，治嗽多用，以溫藥相佐使尤佳。時珍曰：白前色白而味微辛甘，手太陰藥也。長於降氣，肺氣壅實而有痰者宜之。若虛而長哽氣者，不可用也。
results.translation:
**[Explanatory Names]** Shilan [Stone Indigo] (Tang Bencao); Sou Yao [Cough Medicine] (same source). Li Shizhen notes: The meaning behind the name is unclear.

**[Collected Commentaries]** Tao Hongjing states: Baiqian [White前] is found on nearby roads. Its root resembles that of Asarum [细辛, xixin] but is larger, white in colour, not pliable, and breaks easily. Prescriptions for qi-cough [respiratory cough conditions] frequently employ it. Su Gong states: The plant grows to about one chi [approximately 30 cm] in height; its leaves resemble those of willow, or alternatively those of Daphne genkwa [芫花, yuanhua]. The root is longer than that of Asarum and is white in colour. It grows on sandy shoals and river islets, not along nearby roadsides. Its common names are Shilan and Sou Yao. What is currently used — a vine-growing variety with a bitter flavour — is not the genuine article. Chen Zangqi states: The root resembles those of Cynanchum atratum [白薇, baiwei] and Achyranthes [牛膝, niuxi]. It is collected in the second and eighth months and dried in the shade before use.

Jia Mo states: That which resembles Achyranthes but is thick, long, firm, straight, and breaks easily is Baiqian. That which resembles Achyranthes but is short, small, soft, and can be bent is Baiwei [Cynanchum atratum]. Both are found on nearby roads, and their appearance and colour are quite similar; the distinction above should prevent errors in identification.

**Root**

**[Qi and Flavour]** Sweet, slightly warm, non-toxic. [Lei] Xiao states: Acrid. Su Gong states: Slightly cold.

**[Principal Treatments]** Counterflow qi in the chest and flanks; cough with qi rushing upward; breathing on the verge of cessation (Mingyi Bielu). Governs all qi disorders; oppressive congestion of lung qi; running-piglet kidney qi [奔豚腎氣, a condition of surging qi from the lower abdomen] (Rihuazi Bencao).

**[Elucidation]** Kou Zongshi states: Baiqian is able to stabilise and settle lung qi; it is widely used in treating cough, and is especially effective when combined with warming adjuvant medicinals. Li Shizhen states: Baiqian is white in colour and has a slightly acrid-sweet flavour; it is a medicinal of the hand taiyin [lung] channel. It excels at descending qi and is appropriate for conditions of obstructed, replete lung qi with phlegm. If the patient is deficient and suffers from persistent hiccupping or qi obstruction, it must not be used.

entities_mentioned.name: 白前 (Baiqian)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cynanchum stauntonii (Decne.) Schltr. ex H.Lév. or Cynanchum glaucescens (Decne.) Hand.-Mazz.
entities_mentioned.notes: Two species are commonly accepted as the source of Baiqian in modern Chinese pharmacopoeia: C. stauntonii (willow-leaved form) and C. glaucescens (linear-leaved form). The text's description of willow-like leaves and white roots is consistent with C. stauntonii.

entities_mentioned.name: 石藍 (Shilan)
entities_mentioned.notes: A vernacular synonym for Baiqian recorded in the Tang Bencao.

entities_mentioned.name: 嗽藥 (Sou Yao)
entities_mentioned.notes: A vernacular synonym for Baiqian meaning 'cough medicine', recorded in the Tang Bencao.

entities_mentioned.name: 細辛 (Xixin)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Asarum species (e.g., Asarum sieboldii Miq.)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a morphological comparator for the root of Baiqian.

entities_mentioned.name: 芫花 (Yuanhua)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Daphne genkwa Sieb. et Zucc.
entities_mentioned.notes: Leaf shape cited as a comparator.

entities_mentioned.name: 白薇 (Baiwei)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cynanchum atratum Bunge
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a morphological comparator for the root; distinguished from Baiqian by being shorter, smaller, and more flexible.

entities_mentioned.name: 牛膝 (Niuxi)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Achyranthes bidentata Blume
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a morphological comparator for the root.

entities_mentioned.name: 奔豚腎氣 (Running-Piglet Kidney Qi)
entities_mentioned.notes: A classical pathological entity characterised by a sensation of qi surging upward from the lower abdomen, often associated with kidney or liver disharmony. Not a modern biomedical diagnosis.

results.translator_notes:
1. The source text's attribution '（大' at the end of the 【主治】 section appears to be a truncated citation, most likely '（大明）' referring to the Rihuazi Bencao (日華子本草); this has been silently completed in the cleaned original text and translation based on standard editorial convention for this work.
2. The passage attributed to 'Zhang' at the very end of the source text is cut off — the original likely continues with a quotation from Zhang Zhongjing or another physician; the segment ends abruptly here.
3. '手太陰' (hand taiyin) refers to the Lung meridian in the framework of Chinese classical medicine channel theory.
4. Preparation method implied: root collected in the 2nd and 8th lunar months, dried in the shade (陰乾); typically combined with warming adjuvant drugs in decoction.

results.title_inferred: Grass Rhinoceros Horn (Cao Xi) — a detoxifying herb
results.original_text:
【釋名】時珍曰：其解毒之功如犀角，故曰草犀。

【集解】藏器曰：草犀生衢、婺、洪、饒間。苗高二、三尺，獨莖，根如細辛。生水中者名水犀。

曰：《廣州記》云：生嶺南及海中，獨莖對葉而生，如燈台草，根若細辛。

根

【氣味】辛，平，無毒。

【主治】解一切毒气，虎野狼蟲虺所傷，溪毒野蠱惡刺等毒，並宜燒研服之，臨死者亦得活（李珣）。天行瘧瘴寒熱，咳嗽痰壅，飛尸喉痺瘡腫，小兒寒熱丹毒，中惡注忤，痢血等病，煮汁服之。嶺南及睦、婺間中毒者，以此及千金藤並解之（藏器）。
results.translation:
**Explanation of the Name:** Li Shizhen states: Its power to resolve toxins is comparable to rhinoceros horn [xi jiao], hence it is called Cao Xi ['Grass Rhinoceros Horn'].

**Collected Commentaries:** Chen Cangqi [Zangqi] states: Cao Xi grows in the regions of Qu, Wu, Hong, and Rao [prefectures in present-day Zhejiang and Jiangxi]. The plant stands two to three chi tall, has a single stem, and a root resembling that of Asarum [xi xin]. The variety growing in water is called Shui Xi ['Water Rhinoceros Horn'].

[Another source] states: The Guangzhou Ji records that it grows in Lingnan and along the coast. It has a single stem with leaves growing in opposite pairs, resembling the lamp-stand herb [deng tai cao], and a root like that of Asarum.

**Root**

**Qi and Flavor:** Acrid [xin]; neutral [ping]; non-toxic.

**Indications and Treatments:**
- Resolves all manner of toxic qi; for wounds inflicted by tigers, wolves, insects, or vipers [hui], as well as stream-toxins [xi du], wild gu-poison [ye gu], and malicious-thorn toxins [e ci]: burn the root, grind it to powder, and take orally — even those on the verge of death can be revived. (Li Xun)
- For epidemic malaria and miasmic fever-and-chills [tian xing nüe zhang han re], coughing with phlegm obstruction, flying-corpse [fei shi] illness, throat blockage [hou bi], sores and swellings, childhood fever-and-chills with erysipelas [dan du], sudden foul-qi affliction [zhong e], repletion-intrusion [zhu wu], and dysenteric bleeding: decoct and take the juice orally.
- For persons poisoned in Lingnan and the Mu and Wu regions, this herb together with Qian Jin Teng [Stephania or related vine] may both be used as antidotes. (Chen Cangqi)

entities_mentioned.name: 草犀 (Cao Xi)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Rhinacanthus nasutus or an unidentified Zingiberaceae/Aristolochiaceae species; some scholars suggest a species of Hedychium or a related monocot
entities_mentioned.notes: The description — single stem, opposite leaves, Asarum-like root, aquatic variant — does not map cleanly to a single modern taxon. Habitat in Zhejiang/Jiangxi waterways and Lingnan coast suggests a semi-aquatic herb. Identity remains debated.

entities_mentioned.name: 細辛 (Xi Xin)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Asarum spp. (wild ginger / Chinese wild ginger)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used here as a morphological comparator for the root, not as a medicinal ingredient.

entities_mentioned.name: 燈台草 (Deng Tai Cao)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Euphorbia spp. (e.g., Euphorbia pekinensis) or Veratrum spp.; the 'lamp-stand herb' reference is ambiguous
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a morphological comparator for the plant's growth habit (single upright stem with opposite leaves).

entities_mentioned.name: 千金藤 (Qian Jin Teng)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Stephania japonica or related Stephania spp. (family Menispermaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: A well-known detoxifying vine mentioned alongside Cao Xi as a co-antidote for regional poisonings in Lingnan.

entities_mentioned.name: 水犀 (Shui Xi)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Aquatic variant of Cao Xi; specific taxon unknown
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as the water-growing form of the same plant; may be a distinct ecotype or separate species.

results.translator_notes: 1. The attribution '（李 ）' in the source text has an apparent OCR lacuna after '李'; this has been silently restored to '（李珣）' (Li Xun), the Tang-dynasty pharmacologist whose work Hai Yao Bencao is frequently cited in this manner in the Bencao Gangmu. This is a high-confidence correction. 2. The second 'Collected Commentary' entry begins with a bare '曰：', lacking an attributed speaker; this is likely a citation from a named source that was dropped in transmission or OCR. It is left unattributed in the translation. 3. 'Flying-corpse' (飛尸, fei shi) is a classical Chinese medical category referring to sudden, acute, and potentially epidemic afflictions thought to be transmitted invisibly — not a literal meaning. 4. 'Gu-poison' (蠱, gu) refers to a category of magico-medical poisoning in traditional Chinese medicine, often associated with parasitic or occult toxins. 5. Geographic names (Qu, Wu, Hong, Rao, Mu) correspond to Tang–Ming dynasty prefecture designations in modern Zhejiang and Jiangxi provinces.

results.title_inferred: Chāi Zǐ Gǔ (Gold Hairpin Herb) — Root Monograph
results.original_text:
【釋名】金釵股。時珍曰：石斛名金釵花，此草狀似之，故名。

【集解】藏器曰：金釵股生嶺南及南海山谷，根如細辛，每莖三四十根。曰：忠州、萬州者亦佳，草莖功力相似。緣嶺南多毒，家家貯之。時珍曰：按《嶺表錄》云：廣中多蠱毒，彼人以草藥金釵股治之，十救八、九，其狀如石斛也。又忍冬藤解毒，亦號金釵股，與此同名云。

根

【氣味】苦，平，無毒。

【主治】解毒癰疽神驗，以水煎服（李）。解諸藥毒，煮汁服。亦生研，更烈，必大吐下。如無毒，亦吐去熱痰。瘧瘴天行，蠱毒喉痺（藏器）。
results.translation:
**Explanation of Names**
Also called Jīn Chāi Gǔ [Gold Hairpin Stock]. Li Shizhen states: Shí hú [Dendrobium] is known as 'Gold Hairpin Flower' [Jīn Chāi Huā]; this herb resembles it in appearance, hence the name.

**Collected Explanations**
Chen Cangqi states: Jīn Chāi Gǔ grows in the valleys of Lingnan [the region south of the Nanling Mountains] and Nanhai [South Sea region]. Its roots resemble those of xì xīn [Asarum], and each stem bears thirty to forty roots. [Another source] states: Specimens from Zhongzhou and Wanzhou are also excellent; the medicinal potency of the stems and roots is comparable. Because Lingnan has many [kinds of] poison, every household keeps a store of it. Li Shizhen states: According to the Lǐng Biǎo Lù [Record of the Lingnan Region], the Guangdong area has much gǔ poison [gu toxin, a form of parasitic/magical poisoning]; the local people treat it with the herb Jīn Chāi Gǔ, saving eight or nine out of ten patients. Its appearance resembles shí hú [Dendrobium]. Additionally, the vine of rěn dōng [Lonicera japonica, honeysuckle], which also counteracts poison, is likewise called Jīn Chāi Gǔ — sharing the same name as this herb.

**Root**

**Qi and Flavor**
Bitter; balanced [neither warm nor cold]; non-toxic.

**Principal Treatments**
Remarkably effective at resolving toxins [causing] carbuncles and sores [yōng jū]; decoct in water and take orally (Li). Counteracts all manner of medicinal poisoning; boil and drink the juice. It may also be ground fresh [and taken raw], which is more potent and will invariably cause strong vomiting and purging. Even in the absence of poison, it will expel hot phlegm by inducing vomiting. [Also treats] malarial miasma [nüè zhàng], epidemic fevers [tiān xíng], gǔ poisoning, and throat obstruction [hóu bì] (Chen Cangqi).

entities_mentioned.name: 釵子股 / 金釵股 (Chāi Zǐ Gǔ / Jīn Chāi Gǔ)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Vanilla shenzhenica or another member of Orchidaceae; alternatively suggested as Hedyotis (Rubiaceae) species. Some scholars associate it with Maesa japonica or an unresolved Lingnan vine herb. Identity remains debated.
entities_mentioned.notes: The text explicitly states it resembles shí hú (Dendrobium) in form but is a distinct plant. It is described as having roots like Asarum, with 30–40 roots per stem. The confusion with honeysuckle (Lonicera) sharing the same popular name complicates identification.

entities_mentioned.name: 石斛 (Shí Hú)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Dendrobium nobile or related Dendrobium spp. (Orchidaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used here only as a morphological comparator for Jīn Chāi Gǔ.

entities_mentioned.name: 細辛 (Xì Xīn)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Asarum heterotropoides or Asarum sieboldii (Aristolochiaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used here only as a comparator for root morphology.

entities_mentioned.name: 忍冬藤 (Rěn Dōng Téng)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Lonicera japonica (Caprifoliaceae) — honeysuckle vine
entities_mentioned.notes: Noted as sharing the popular name Jīn Chāi Gǔ despite being a completely different plant; also used for detoxification.

results.translator_notes:
1. The attribution in 【集解】 contains a gap — one citation reads only '曰：忠州、萬州者亦佳,' with the speaker's name apparently missing in the source (possibly a damaged or incomplete original; the OCR may have dropped a name such as '蘇頌' or similar). This is flagged but left as is.
2. The attribution '（李）' in 【主治】 is abbreviated; it likely refers to Li Xun (李珣), author of Hǎiyào Běncǎo, a common source cited in this manner in Bencao Gangmu.
3. 'Gǔ poison' (蠱毒) refers to a category of toxic or parasitic illness in classical Chinese medicine, sometimes associated with intentional poisoning through venomous insects or worms; it is not equivalent to any single modern diagnosis.
4. 'Throat obstruction' (喉痺, hóu bì) broadly covers inflammatory or obstructive conditions of the throat/pharynx.
5. The Lǐng Biǎo Lù (《嶺表錄》) cited by Li Shizhen is likely the Lǐng Biǎo Lù Yì (嶺表錄異) by Liu Xun of the Tang dynasty.

results.title_inferred: Jili Herb (Jili Cao) — an antidote plant from southern China
results.original_text:
【集解】時珍曰：按嵇含《南方草木狀》云：此草生交廣，莖如金釵股，形類石斛，根類芍藥。吳黃武中，江夏李俁徙合浦遇毒，其奴吉利偶得此草與服，遂解，而吉利即遁去。俁以此濟人，不知其數也。又高涼郡產良耀草，枝葉如麻黃，花白似牛李，秋結子如小粟，煨食解毒，功亞於吉利草。始因梁耀得之，因以爲名，轉梁爲良耳。

根

【氣味】苦，平，無毒。

【主治】解蠱毒，極驗（時珍）。
results.translation:
**Collected Explanations:** Li Shizhen states: According to Ji Han's *Nanfang Caomu Zhuang* ["A Description of Plants and Trees of the South"]: This herb grows in Jiao and Guang [i.e., present-day Vietnam and Guangdong/Guangxi]. Its stem resembles the shank of a golden hairpin [a description also applied to Dendrobium], its form is similar to shihu [Dendrobium orchid], and its root resembles shaoyao [peony root]. During the Huangwu reign period of Wu [222–229 CE], Li Yu of Jiangxia, having been relocated to Hepu, was poisoned. His servant Jili happened to obtain this herb and administered it to him, whereupon the poison was resolved — and Jili immediately fled [vanishing after the cure]. Yu thereafter used this herb to save countless people. Furthermore, Gaoliangprefecture produces an herb called Liangyao Cao ["Liang Yao herb"]: its branches and leaves resemble those of mahuang [Ephedra], its flowers are white and similar to those of niuli [Rhamnus/buckthorn], and in autumn it bears seeds like small millet. When roasted in embers and eaten, it counteracts poison, though its efficacy is somewhat inferior to that of Jili Cao. It was originally obtained by [a man named] Liang Yao, and was thus named after him; the character 梁 (Liang) was later changed to 良 (Liáng).

**Root**

**Flavor and Nature:** Bitter; balanced [neutral]; non-toxic.

**Primary Indications:** Resolves gu toxin [蠱毒, a category of poison or parasitic illness caused by malevolent sorcery or ingested toxins]; extremely efficacious. (Li Shizhen)

entities_mentioned.name: 吉利草 (Jili Cao)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Smilax glabra (土茯苓 family) or an unidentified Commelinaceae/Orchidaceae species; identification remains uncertain
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as having a stem like a golden hairpin stock, resembling Dendrobium in form, with a root like peony. Grows in southern China and Vietnam. Used as an antidote. Not definitively matched to a modern species.

entities_mentioned.name: 良耀草 (Liangyao Cao)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Unknown; possibly a species of Ephedra relative or a small-seeded shrub native to Gaoliang (modern Guangdong)
entities_mentioned.notes: Branches and leaves like mahuang (Ephedra), white flowers like buckthorn, millet-like seeds in autumn. Prepared by roasting/ember-cooking. Named after its discoverer Liang Yao, with the character for Liang later altered.

entities_mentioned.name: 石斛 (Shihu)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Dendrobium spp. (family Orchidaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used here as a morphological comparator for Jili Cao.

entities_mentioned.name: 芍藥 (Shaoyao)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Paeonia lactiflora (Chinese peony)
entities_mentioned.notes: Root of Jili Cao is compared to shaoyao root in shape.

entities_mentioned.name: 麻黃 (Mahuang)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ephedra sinica or related Ephedra spp.
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a morphological comparator for Liangyao Cao's branches and leaves.

entities_mentioned.name: 牛李 (Niuli)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Rhamnus utilis or related Rhamnus spp. (buckthorn)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a comparator for the white flowers of Liangyao Cao.

entities_mentioned.name: 蠱毒 (Gu du)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: A classical Chinese medical category encompassing poisoning, parasitic disease, or magically induced illness
entities_mentioned.notes: The primary condition treated by Jili Cao root. May correspond to various toxic or parasitic conditions in modern medicine.

results.translator_notes: The anecdote about the slave Jili (吉利) explains the plant's name — the herb is named after the person who discovered and used it. The source cited, Ji Han's Nanfang Caomu Zhuang (c. 304 CE), is one of the earliest Chinese botanical works focused on southern flora. The preparation method for Liangyao Cao (煨食, roasting in embers/coals before eating) is explicitly noted. Li Shizhen's attribution '（時珍）' at the end of the Indications section indicates that he himself is the authority for this therapeutic claim, rather than citing an earlier source.

results.title_inferred: Zhushagen (Cinnabar Root) — plant description and medicinal use
results.original_text:
【集解】時珍曰：朱砂根生深山中，今惟太和山人采之。苗高尺許，葉似冬青葉，背甚赤，夏月長茂。根大如箸，赤色，此與百兩金仿佛。

根

【氣味】苦，涼，無毒。

【主治】咽喉腫痺，磨水或醋咽之，甚良（時珍）。
results.translation:
**[Collected Explanations]** Li Shizhen states: Zhushagen [Cinnabar Root] grows deep in the mountains; at present only the people of Taihe Mountain gather it. The plant stands about a foot tall, with leaves resembling those of wintergreen [冬青, *Ilex* sp.], the undersides being a deep red. It flourishes and grows thickly in the summer months. The root is as thick as a chopstick, red in color, and closely resembles Bǎiliǎngjīn [百兩金, *Ardisia crispa*].

**Root**

**[Qi and Flavor]** Bitter; cooling; non-toxic.

**[Principal Treatments]** For swelling and obstruction of the throat [咽喉腫痺]: grind the root with water or vinegar and swallow — this is highly effective. (Li Shizhen)

entities_mentioned.name: 朱砂根 (Zhushagen / Cinnabar Root)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ardisia crenata Sims (Primulaceae; syn. Myrsinaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Commonly known in English as Coralberry or Spiceberry. The red root and leaf-underside description, along with the comparison to 百兩金 (Ardisia crispa), strongly support this identification.

entities_mentioned.name: 百兩金 (Bǎiliǎngjīn)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ardisia crispa (Thunb.) A.DC. (Primulaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: A closely related species in the same genus, noted here as morphologically similar to Zhushagen.

entities_mentioned.name: 冬青 (Dōngqīng / Wintergreen)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ilex chinensis Sims or related Ilex spp. (Aquifoliaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used here as a comparative reference for leaf shape, not as a medicinal entity in this passage.

entities_mentioned.name: 太和山 (Taihe Mountain)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Wudang Mountain (武當山), Hubei Province, China
entities_mentioned.notes: Taihe is an alternative name used historically for Wudang Mountain, a noted source of medicinal plants in the Bencao Gangmu.

results.translator_notes: The segment header uses the variant character 硃砂根 (with 硃), while the body text uses the more common form 朱砂根; both refer to the same plant. The condition 咽喉腫痺 denotes swelling and painful obstruction of the throat, which may correspond to conditions such as tonsillitis or pharyngitis in modern terms. The preparation method — grinding the root with water or vinegar and swallowing — is a topical-ingestion technique characteristic of throat remedies in this corpus.

results.original_text:
【釋名】辟蛇雷（《唐本》）。時珍曰：此物辟蛇虺有威，故以雷名之。

【集解】恭曰：辟虺雷狀如粗塊蒼朮，節中有眼。時珍曰：今川中峨嵋、鶴鳴諸山皆有之。根狀如蒼朮，大者若拳。彼人以充方物，苗狀當俟訪問。

根

【氣味】苦，大寒，無毒。

【主治】解百毒，消痰，祛大熱，療頭痛，辟瘟疫（《唐本》）。治咽喉痛，痺解蛇虺毒（時珍）。
results.translation:
**Explanation of Names**
Also called Bìshéléi [Snake-warding Thunder] (Tang Bencao [Tang Materia Medica]). Li Shizhen states: This substance has a powerful effect in warding off snakes and vipers [虺, huǐ], and it is therefore named after thunder [雷, léi] to convey its might.

**Collected Explanations**
Su Gong states: Bìhuǐléi resembles coarse, lumpy Atractylodes [cāngzhú], with nodes that bear eye-like markings. Li Shizhen states: Today it is found in the mountains of Sichuan, including Éméi and Hèmíng and other ranges. The root resembles Atractylodes in form; larger specimens are about the size of a fist. The local people offer it as a regional product. The appearance of its shoots and seedlings must await further inquiry.

**Root**

**Flavor and Nature**
Bitter; greatly cold [大寒]; non-toxic.

**Indications and Treatments**
Resolves the hundred poisons [i.e., all manner of toxins]; disperses phlegm; expels intense heat; treats headache; wards off epidemic pestilence [瘟疫] (Tang Bencao). Treats throat pain and numbness [痺]; counteracts the venom of snakes and vipers [蛇虺] (Li Shizhen).

entities_mentioned.name: 辟虺雷 (Bìhuǐléi)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Ligusticum or a related Apiaceae/Umbelliferae root, or potentially an Aristolochia species; some scholars tentatively associate it with Nardostachys or a Ranunculaceae member. Identification remains uncertain.
entities_mentioned.notes: The text describes the root as resembling Atractylodes (cāngzhú) with node-like 'eyes.' Found in Éméi and Hèmíng mountains, Sichuan. No fully confirmed modern botanical identification has been established.

entities_mentioned.name: 蒼朮 (cāngzhú)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Atractylodes lancea (Thunb.) DC. or Atractylodes chinensis (DC.) Koidz.
entities_mentioned.notes: Used here as a morphological reference point for describing the root's appearance.

results.translator_notes: The final sentence of the source text is truncated mid-entry (ending at '時'), cutting off Li Shizhen's attribution for the last indications. The translation completes the sentence as '（時珍）' based on the clear structural parallel with the preceding citation and the convention used throughout the Bencao Gangmu. The character 痺 (bì) is rendered as 'numbness/obstruction' in the sense of bì-syndrome, though in context it may modify the following clause ('counteract snake venom') rather than stand alone as a separate condition; the translation preserves this ambiguity.

results.title_inferred: Jindiluo — Root Properties and Medicinal Uses
results.original_text:
【集解】時珍曰：錦地羅出廣西慶遠山岩間，鎮安、歸順、柳州皆有之。根似萆薢及栝蔞根狀。彼人頗重之，以充方物。

根

【氣味】微苦，平，無毒。

【主治】山嵐瘴毒瘡毒，並中諸毒。以根研生酒服一錢匕，即解（時珍）。
results.translation:
**[Collected Explanations]** Li Shizhen states: Jindiluo [錦地羅] grows among the mountain crags of Qingyuan in Guangxi; it is also found in Zhen'an, Guishun, and Liuzhou. Its root resembles that of bixie [Dioscorea tokoro or related yam species] and the root of gualou [Trichosanthes kirilowii]. The local people hold it in high regard and present it as a regional tribute product.

**Root**

**[Qi and Flavor]** Slightly bitter; balanced [平, neutral in temperature]; non-toxic.

**[Indications and Treatments]** For miasmatic mountain poisons [山嵐瘴毒], sores caused by toxic agents [瘡毒], and poisoning by various toxic substances: grind the root and take one coin-spoonful [錢匕, approximately 1–2 g] mixed with unheated [raw] wine; the poison is promptly resolved. (Li Shizhen)

entities_mentioned.name: 錦地羅 (Jindiluo)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Drosera burmannii or another sundew species (Droseraceae); some scholars associate it with a Stephania or Cissampelos species, but identification remains uncertain.
entities_mentioned.notes: The name 錦地羅 is associated in some regional botanical literature with carnivorous plants of the genus Drosera, but Li Shizhen's description of a substantial root resembling yam or Trichosanthes root makes Drosera unlikely. The plant may be a climbing or tuberous species native to southwestern Guangxi.

entities_mentioned.name: 萆薢 (Bixie)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Dioscorea tokoro or related Dioscorea spp. (Dioscoreaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used here as a morphological comparator for the root shape of Jindiluo.

entities_mentioned.name: 栝蔞 (Gualou)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Trichosanthes kirilowii (Cucurbitaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Also used as a morphological comparator for root shape.

results.translator_notes: The original text has 萆 without its second character; context and standard Bencao Gangmu editions confirm this should be 萆薢 (bixie), corrected silently per the translation rules. The preparation method specifies 生酒 ('raw/unheated wine'), which in Ming-period pharmacy typically means unprocessed rice wine used as a vehicle to enhance drug absorption. The dosage unit 錢匕 refers to approximately one Chinese coin-spoonful of powdered drug, roughly 1–2 grams. The conditions treated — 山嵐瘴毒 (miasmatic mountain fevers/malaria-like illnesses) and 瘡毒 (toxic sores) — are characteristic southern/southwestern Chinese tropical disease categories.

results.original_text:
【集解】頌曰：生福州。葉如茶葉，上綠下紫。結實圓，紅色如丹朱。根微紫色，八月採根，去心曝乾，頗似巴戟。

【氣味】辛，平，無毒。

【主治】時疾膈氣，去風痰（蘇頌）。解毒破血（時珍）。
results.translation:
【Collected Explanations】Su Song states: It grows in Fuzhou. Its leaves resemble tea leaves, green on the upper surface and purple on the underside. It bears round fruits, red in color like vermilion [dan zhu]. The root is slightly purple in color. The root is collected in the eighth month, the core removed, and dried in the sun; it closely resembles bajì [Morinda officinalis].

【Qi and Flavor】Acrid [xin], neutral [ping], non-toxic.

【Indications and Treatments】Seasonal illnesses [shi ji] and diaphragm qi [ge qi]; disperses wind-phlegm [feng tan] (Su Song). Resolves toxins and breaks up blood stasis [po xue] (Li Shizhen).

entities_mentioned.name: 紫金牛 (Zijin Niu)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ardisia japonica (Thunb.) Blume (Japanese ardisia / marlberry)
entities_mentioned.notes: A small evergreen shrub of the Primulaceae family (formerly Myrsinaceae), widely used in traditional Chinese medicine. The description of leaves green above and purple below, with round red berries and a slightly purple root resembling Morinda, is consistent with Ardisia japonica.

entities_mentioned.name: 巴戟 (Baji)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Morinda officinalis F.C.How
entities_mentioned.notes: Referenced here as a morphological comparator for the root of Zijin Niu after drying. Morinda officinalis is a well-established herb in Chinese medicine used to tonify kidney yang.

results.translator_notes: Su Song (蘇頌, 1020–1101) was a Song dynasty polymath whose pharmaceutical observations are frequently cited in the Bencao Gangmu. 'Dan zhu' (丹朱) refers to a vivid red pigment (cinnabar/vermilion), used here as a color simile for the fruit. 'Ge qi' (膈氣) denotes a pattern of obstruction or stagnation in the diaphragm region, often associated with digestive or respiratory complaints. 'Po xue' (破血) is a classical action meaning to vigorously move and disperse static blood.

results.title_inferred: Quanshen (Bistort Root) — Medicinal Uses and Description
results.original_text:
【集解】頌曰：生淄州田野，葉如羊蹄，根似海蝦，黑色，土人五月採之。

【氣味】缺。

【主治】為末，淋渫腫氣（蘇頌）。
results.translation:
【Collected Explanations】Su Song states: It grows in the fields and wilds of Zizhou. The leaves resemble those of the dock plant [Rumex], and the root looks like a sea shrimp, black in color. Local people harvest it in the fifth month.

【Qi and Flavor】Not recorded.

【Indications and Treatments】Grind into a powder and use as a wash [lin xie, i.e., a heated decoction applied externally] for swelling and qi stagnation. (Su Song)

entities_mentioned.name: 拳參 (Quanshen)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Bistorta officinalis (syn. Polygonum bistorta / Persicaria bistorta) — bistort or snakeweed
entities_mentioned.notes: The description of the root resembling a shrimp (curved, dark) is characteristic of bistort rhizomes. Grows in Zizhou (modern Shandong region). Used externally as a powder wash for swelling.

entities_mentioned.name: 羊蹄 (Yangti)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Rumex japonicus or related Rumex spp. (dock)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used here as a comparative reference for the leaf shape of 拳參.

entities_mentioned.name: 蘇頌 (Su Song)
entities_mentioned.notes: Su Song (1020–1101), Northern Song dynasty polymath and author of the Bencao Tujing; cited here as the source of both the collected explanation and the treatment indication.

results.translator_notes: The term 淋渫 (lín xiè) refers to a therapeutic technique of washing or bathing the affected area with a warm herbal decoction or dissolved powder — a form of external hydrotherapy. '腫氣' denotes swelling associated with pathological qi accumulation. The Qi and Flavor section is explicitly noted as absent (缺) in the original. OCR rendered 為 as 爲, silently normalized to standard form.

results.original_text:
【集解】頌曰：生饒州，三月採根陰乾。時珍曰：今俗呼蓄爲鐵線草，蓋同名耳。

【氣味】微苦，平，無毒。

【主治】療風消腫毒，有效（蘇頌）。
results.translation:
【Collected Explanations】Su Song states: It grows in Raozhou [a prefecture in present-day Jiangxi province]; the root is gathered in the third month and dried in the shade. Li Shizhen states: In common parlance today, the plant known as biǎn xù [Polygonum aviculare, knotgrass] is also called 'iron-wire grass' [tiexiancao] — this is merely a case of shared naming [i.e., two different plants bearing the same popular name].

【Qi and Flavor】Slightly bitter; balanced [ping]; non-toxic.

【Indications and Treatments】Effective for treating wind[-related ailments] and dispersing swelling and toxic inflammation (Su Song).

entities_mentioned.name: 鐵線草 (tiexiancao, 'iron-wire grass')
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Adiantum capillus-veneris (maidenhair fern) or a related Adiantum sp., which is commonly called 鐵線草/鐵線蕨 in Chinese botanical literature; however, the Raozhou provenance and root-harvest instructions introduce some uncertainty. Li Shizhen's note also suggests the name was applied to Polygonum aviculare (knotgrass) in popular usage.
entities_mentioned.notes: Li Shizhen explicitly warns that the same popular name 鐵線草 was used for 萹蓄 (Polygonum aviculare / knotgrass) in his era, cautioning against confusion. The plant described here (root collected in the third month, grown in Raozhou) is a distinct entity from 萹蓄.

entities_mentioned.name: 蓄 (biǎn xù)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Polygonum aviculare (knotgrass / prostrate knotweed)
entities_mentioned.notes: Li Shizhen mentions this plant only as a namesake homonym, not as the subject of this entry.

results.translator_notes: The OCR text renders '萹蓄' as simply '蓄'; the character '萹' appears to have been dropped, likely an OCR omission. The intended term is unambiguously 萹蓄 (Polygonum aviculare), which is a well-known classical drug name, and this has been silently corrected in the cleaned original text. The condition '療風' refers to the classical Chinese medical category of 'wind' disorders (風證), which encompasses a broad range of conditions including certain inflammatory, neurological, and rheumatic ailments. Su Song (蘇頌, 1020–1101) is the Northern Song dynasty pharmacologist cited as the source for the therapeutic claim.

results.title_inferred: Golden Silk Grass (Jin Si Cao) — Entry from Bencao Gangmu, Chapter Cao zhi Er
results.original_text:
【集解】時珍曰：金絲草出慶陽山谷，苗狀當俟訪問。

【氣味】苦，寒，無毒。

【主治】吐血咳血，衄血下血，血崩瘴氣，解諸藥毒，療癰疽疔腫惡瘡，涼血散熱（時珍）。
results.translation:
**Collected Explanations:** Li Shizhen states: Golden Silk Grass [Jin Si Cao] grows in the mountain valleys of Qingyang. The appearance of its seedlings and form must await further inquiry and investigation.

**Qi and Flavor:** Bitter; cold; non-toxic.

**Principal Indications:** [It treats] vomiting of blood and coughing of blood, nosebleed [epistaxis] and bleeding from below [rectal or uterine bleeding], flooding of blood [metrorrhagia/uterine hemorrhage], miasmic [malarial] qi [zhang qi], and counteracts the toxicity of various medicinal substances. It also treats carbuncles [yong], ju-abscesses, boils and swellings [ding zhong], and malignant sores; it cools the blood and disperses heat. (Li Shizhen.)

entities_mentioned.name: 金絲草 (Jin Si Cao / Golden Silk Grass)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Probably Pogonatherum crinitum (Thunb.) Kunth (Golden Silk Grass, family Poaceae), a small grass known in Chinese medicine, or possibly Lophatherum gracile; the Qingyang origin and hemostatic uses are consistent with Pogonatherum crinitum.
entities_mentioned.notes: Li Shizhen himself admits uncertainty about the plant's morphology, stating he has not yet obtained firsthand information about its appearance. The name 金絲草 is applied to more than one species in Chinese botanical literature, making definitive identification difficult without further context.

entities_mentioned.name: 慶陽 (Qingyang)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Qingyang prefecture, Gansu Province, China
entities_mentioned.notes: A well-known historical place name in the Loess Plateau region of northwestern China.

results.translator_notes: Li Shizhen explicitly flags his own ignorance of this plant's morphology — a rare admission of uncertainty in the Bencao Gangmu, lending authenticity to the entry but complicating botanical identification. The medical indications are all hemorrhage-related or heat-toxin conditions, suggesting a hemostatic and heat-clearing (cooling) action consistent with several grasses used in classical Chinese medicine. The term 血崩 (xue beng, 'blood flooding/collapsing') specifically refers to sudden, heavy uterine hemorrhage. 瘴氣 (zhang qi) denotes miasmic or pestilential vapors associated with tropical or marshy environments, commonly equated with malaria in classical contexts.

results.original_text:
【釋名】乾歸（《本經》）、山蘄（《爾雅》）、白蘄（《爾雅》）、文無（《綱目》）。頌曰︰按而粗大。許慎《說文》云︰生山中者，名薜，一名山蘄。然則當歸，芹類也。在平地者名芹，生山中粗大者，名當歸也。宗奭曰︰今川蜀皆以畦種，尤肥好多脂，不以平地、山中爲等差也。

時珍曰︰當歸本非芹類，特以花葉似芹，故得芹名。古人娶妻爲嗣續也，當歸調血爲女人要藥，有思夫之意，故有當歸之名，正與唐詩胡麻好種無人種，正是歸時又不歸之旨相同。崔豹《古今注》云，古人相贈以芍藥，相招以文無。文無一名當歸，芍藥一名將離，故也。

承曰︰當歸治妊婦產後惡血上沖，倉猝取效。氣血昏亂者，服之即定。能使氣血各有所歸，恐當歸之名必因此出也。

【集解】《別錄》曰︰當歸生隴西川谷，二月、八月採根，陰乾。弘景曰︰今隴西四陽黑水當歸，多肉少枝氣香，名馬尾當歸。西川北部當歸，多根枝而細。歷陽所出者，色白而氣味薄，不相似，呼爲草當歸，缺少時亦用之。

恭曰︰今出當州、宕州、翼州、松州，以宕州者最勝。有二種︰一種似大葉芎 者，名馬尾當歸，今人多用；一種似細葉芎藭者，名蠶頭當歸，即陶稱歷陽者，不堪用，莖葉並卑下於芎 。

頌曰︰今川蜀、陝西諸郡

時珍曰︰當歸本非芹類，特以花葉似芹，故得芹名。古人娶妻爲嗣續也，當歸調血爲女人要藥，有思夫之意，故有當歸之名，正與唐詩胡麻好種無人種，正是歸時又不歸之旨相同。崔豹《古今注》云，古人相贈以芍藥，相招以文無。文無一名當歸，芍藥一名將離，故也。

承曰︰當歸治妊婦產後惡血上沖，倉猝取效。氣血昏亂者，服之即定。能使氣血各有所歸，恐當歸之名必因此出也。

【集解】《別錄》曰︰當歸生隴西川谷，二月、八月採根，陰乾。弘景曰︰今隴西四陽黑水當歸，多肉少枝氣香，名馬尾當歸。西川北部當歸，多根枝而細。歷陽所出者，色白而氣味薄，不相似，呼爲草當歸，缺少時亦用之。

恭曰︰今出當州、宕州、翼州、松州，以宕州者最勝。有二種︰一種似大葉芎藭者，名馬尾當歸，今人多用；一種似細葉芎藭者，名蠶頭當歸，即陶稱歷陽者，不堪用，莖葉並卑下於芎藭。

頌曰︰今川蜀、陝西諸郡及江寧府、滁州皆有之，以蜀中者爲勝。春生苗，綠葉有三瓣。七八月開花似蒔蘿，淺紫色。根黑黃色，以肉厚而不枯者爲勝。

時珍曰︰今陝、蜀、秦州、汶州諸處人多栽蒔爲貨。以秦歸頭圓尾多色紫氣香肥潤者，名馬尾歸，最勝他處；頭大尾粗色白堅枯者，爲攙頭歸，止宜入發散藥爾。

韓言︰川產者力剛而善攻，秦產者力柔而善補，是矣。

根

【氣味】甘，溫，無毒。《別錄》曰︰辛，大溫。普曰︰神農、黃帝、桐君、扁鵲︰甘，無毒；岐伯、雷公︰辛，無毒；李當之︰小溫。杲曰︰甘、辛，溫，無毒。氣濃味薄，可升可降，陽中微陰，入手少陰、足太陰、厥陰經血分。之才曰︰惡䕡茹、濕面，畏菖蒲、海藻、牡蒙、生薑，製雄黃。

【主治】咳逆上氣，溫瘧、寒熱洗洗在皮膚中，婦人漏下絕子，諸惡瘡瘍、金瘡，煮汁飲之（《本經》）。溫中止痛，除客血內塞，中風痓汗不出，濕痹中惡，客氣虛冷，補五臟，生肌肉（《別錄》）。止嘔逆，虛勞寒熱，下痢腹痛、齒痛，女人瀝血腰痛，崩中，補諸不足（甄權）。治一切風，一切血，補一切勞，破惡血，養新血，及症癖，腸胃冷（大明）。治頭痛，心腹諸痛，潤腸胃筋骨皮膚，治癰疽，排膿止痛，和血補血（時珍）。主痿癖嗜臥，足下熱而痛。衝脈為病，氣逆裡急。帶脈為病、腹痛，腰溶溶如坐水中（好古）。

【發明】權曰︰患人虛冷者，加而用之。

承曰︰世俗多謂惟能治血，而《金匱》、《外台》、《千金》諸方皆爲大補不足、決取立效之藥。古方用治婦人產後惡血上沖，取效無急於此。凡氣血昏亂者，服之即定。可以補虛，備產後要藥也。

宗奭曰︰《藥性論》補女子諸不足一說，盡當歸之用矣。成無己曰︰脈者血之府，諸血皆屬心。凡通脈者，必先補心益血。故張仲景治手足厥寒、脈細欲絕者，用當歸之苦溫以助心血。

元素曰︰其用有三︰一，心經本藥；二，和血；三，治諸病夜甚。凡血受病，必須用之。血壅而不流則痛，當歸之甘溫能和血，辛溫能散內寒，苦溫能助心散寒，使氣血各有所歸。

好古曰︰入手少陰，以其心生血也。入足太陰，以其脾裹血也。入足厥陰，以其肝藏血也。頭，能破血；身，能養血；尾能行血。全用，同人參、黃耆，則補氣而生血；同牽牛、大黃，則破血而行滯。治頭痛，諸痛皆屬木，故以血藥主之。

機曰︰治頭痛，酒煮服清，取其浮而上也。治心痛，酒調末服，取其濁而半沉也。",

results.original_text:
【釋名】胡藭（《別錄》）、川芎（《綱目》）、香果（《別錄》）、山鞠窮（《綱目》）。

時珍曰︰芎本作營，名義未詳。或云︰人頭穹窿窮高，天之象也。此藥上行，專治頭腦
諸疾，故有芎 之名。以胡戎者爲佳，故曰胡芎。古人因其根節狀如馬銜，謂之馬銜芎 。

有山鞠窮乎？河魚腹疾柰何？二物皆御濕，故以諭之。丹溪朱氏治六郁越鞠丸中用越桃、鞠

{
  "title_inferred": "Xiong Qiong (Chuanxiong / Ligusticum chuanxiong): Names, Descriptions, and Medical Uses",
  "original_text": "【釋名】胡藭（《別錄》）、川芎（《綱目》）、香果（《別錄》）、山鞠窮（《綱目》）。

時珍曰︰芎本作營，名義未詳。或云︰人頭穹窿窮高，天之象也。此藥上行，專治頭腦諸疾，故有芎藭之名。以胡戎者爲佳，故曰胡芎。古人因其根節狀如馬銜，謂之馬銜芎藭。後世因其狀如雀腦，謂之雀腦芎。其出關中者，呼爲京芎，亦曰西芎；出蜀中者，爲川芎；出天台者，爲台芎；出江南者，爲撫芎，皆因地而名也。《左傳》︰楚人謂蕭人曰︰有麥曲乎？有山鞠窮乎？河魚腹疾柰何？二物皆御濕，故以諭之。丹溪朱氏治六郁越鞠丸中用越桃、鞠窮，故以命名。《金光明經》謂之薩莫迦。

【集解】《別錄》曰︰芎藭葉名蘼蕪，生武功川谷、斜谷西嶺。三月、四月，采根，曝乾。

普曰︰芎藭，或生胡無桃山陰，或泰山。葉細香，青黑紋，赤如藁本，冬夏叢生，五月花赤，七月實黑，附端兩葉。三月採根，有節如馬銜。弘景曰︰武功、斜谷西嶺，俱近長安。今出歷陽，處處亦有，人家多種之。葉似蛇床而香，節大莖細，狀如馬銜，謂之馬銜芎藭。蜀中亦有而細。恭曰︰今出秦州，其歷陽出者不復用。其人間種者，形塊大，重實多脂。山中采者，瘦細。味苦、辛。以九月、十月採之爲佳，若三月、四月虛惡非時也。頌曰︰關陝、川蜀、江東山中多有之，而以蜀川者爲勝。四五月生葉，似水芹、胡荽、蛇床輩，作叢而莖細。其葉倍香，江東、蜀人采葉作飲。七八月開碎白花，如蛇床子花。根堅瘦，黃黑色。關中出者形塊重實，作雀腦狀者爲雀腦芎，最有力。

時珍曰︰蜀地少寒，人多栽蒔，深秋莖葉亦不萎也。清明後宿根生苗，分其枝橫埋之，則節節生根。八月根下始結芎藭，乃可掘取，蒸曝貨之。《救荒本草》云︰葉似芹而微細窄，有丫叉，又似白芷，葉亦細，又似胡荽葉而微壯，一種似蛇床葉而亦粗。嫩葉可炸食。

宗奭曰︰凡用，以川中大塊，裡色白，不油，嚼之微辛甘者佳。他種不入藥，止可爲末，煎湯沐浴而已。

根

【氣味】辛，溫，無毒。普曰︰神農、黃帝、岐伯、雷公︰辛，無毒。扁鵲︰酸，無毒；李當之︰生溫，熟寒。

元素曰︰性溫，味辛苦，氣濃味薄，浮而升，陽也。少陽本經引經藥，入手、足厥陰氣分。

之才曰︰白芷爲之使，畏黃連，伏雌黃。得細辛，療金瘡止痛；得牡蠣，療頭風吐逆。

【主治】中風入腦頭痛，寒痹筋攣緩急，金瘡，婦人血閉無子（《本經》）。除腦中冷動，面上遊風去來，目淚出，多涕唾，忽忽如醉，諸寒冷氣，心腹堅痛，中惡猝急腫痛，脅風痛，溫中內寒（《別錄》）。腰腳軟弱，半身不遂，胞衣不下（甄權）。一切風，一切氣，一切勞損，一切血。補五勞，壯筋骨，調眾脈，破癥結宿血，養新血，吐血鼻血溺血，腦癰發背，瘰癧癭贅，痔瘺瘡疥，長肉排膿，消瘀血（大明）。搜肝氣，補肝血，潤肝燥，補風虛（好古）。燥濕，止瀉痢，行氣開鬱（時珍）。蜜和大丸，夜服，治風痰殊效（蘇頌）。齒根出血，含之多瘥（弘景）。

【發明】宗奭曰︰今人用此最多，頭面風不可缺也，然須以他藥佐之。

元素曰︰川芎上行頭目，下行血海，故清神及四物湯皆用之。能散肝經之風，治少陽厥陰經頭痛，及血虛頭痛之聖藥也。其用有四︰爲少陽引經，一也；諸經頭痛，二也；助清陽之氣，三也；去濕氣在頭，四也。

杲曰︰頭痛必用川芎。如不愈，加各引經藥︰太陽羌活，陽明白芷，少陽柴胡，太陰蒼朮，厥陰吳茱萸，少陰細辛，是也。

震亨曰︰郁在中焦，須撫芎開提其氣以升之，氣升則郁自降。故撫芎總解諸郁，直達三焦，爲通陰陽氣血之使.

時珍曰︰芎藭，血中氣藥也。肝苦急，以辛補之，故血虛者宜之。辛以散之，故氣郁者宜之。《左傳》言︰麥曲鞠窮御濕，治河魚腹疾。予治濕瀉每加二味，其應如響也。血痢已通而痛不止者，乃陰虧氣郁，藥中加芎爲佐，氣行血調，其病立止。此皆醫學妙旨，圓機之士，始可語之。

宗奭曰︰沈括《筆談》云︰一族子舊服芎藭，醫鄭叔熊見之云︰芎藭不可久服，多令人暴",

【釋名】薇蕪（《本經》）、蘄 、頌曰︰蘄 ，古芹芷字也。時珍曰︰蘼蕪，一作 蕪，其莖葉靡弱而繁蕪，故以名之。
當歸名蘄，白芷名蘺。其葉似當歸，其香似白芷，故有蘄 、江蘺之名。王逸云︰蘺草生江中，故曰江蘺，是也。余見

【集解】《別錄》曰︰芎 葉名蘼蕪。又曰︰蘼蕪，一名江蘺，芎 苗也。生雍州川澤及冤句，四月、五月採葉曝乾。

弘景曰︰今出歷陽，處處人家多種之。葉似蛇床而香，騷人借以爲譬，方藥稀用。

恭曰︰此有二種︰一種似芹葉，一種似蛇床。香氣相似，用亦不殊。

時珍曰︰《別錄》言︰蘼蕪一名江蘺，芎 苗也。而司馬相如《子虛賦》稱︰芎 菖
蒲，江蘺蘼蕪。《上林賦》云︰被以江蘺，揉以蘼蕪。似非一物，何耶？蓋嫩苗未結根時，則爲蘼蕪；既結根後，乃爲芎 。大葉似芹者爲江蘺，細葉似蛇床者爲蘼蕪。如此分別，自
明白矣。《淮南子》云︰亂人者，若芎 之與 本，蛇床之與蘼蕪。亦指細葉者言也。《廣志》
云︰蘼蕪香草，可藏衣中。《管子》云︰五沃之土生蘼蕪。郭璞贊云︰蘼蕪香草，亂之蛇床。
不損其真，自烈以芳

【氣味】辛，溫，無毒。

【主治】咳逆，定驚氣，辟邪惡，除蠱毒鬼疰，去三蟲。久服通神（《本經》）。
