results.original_text:
【釋名】蓬莪朮。

【集解】志曰：蓬莪朮生西戎及廣南諸州。葉似荷，子似乾椹，朮在根下並生，一好一惡，惡者有毒。西戎人取之，先放羊食，羊不食者棄之。

藏器曰：一名蓬莪，黑色；二名（莪），黃色；三名波殺，味甘。大明曰：即南中薑黃根也。海南生者名蓬莪朮。

頌曰：今江浙或有之，三月生苗，在田野中。其莖如錢大，高二、三尺。葉青白色，長一、二尺，大五寸以來，頗類荷。五月有花作穗，黃色，頭微紫。根如生薑，而朮在根下，似雞鴨卵。

【氣味】苦、辛，溫，無毒。

大明曰：得酒、醋良。

【主治】心腹痛，中惡疰忤鬼氣，霍亂冷氣，吐酸水，解毒，食飲不消，酒研服之。又療婦人血氣結積，丈夫奔豚（《開寶》）。破癖冷氣，以酒醋磨服（甄權）。治一切氣，開胃消食，通月經，消瘀血，止撲損痛下血，及內損惡血（大明）。通肝經聚血（好古）。

【發明】頌曰：蓬莪朮，古方不見用者。今醫家治積聚諸氣，為最要之藥。與荊三棱同用之良，婦人藥中亦多使。

好古曰：蓬莪色黑，破氣中之血，入氣藥發諸香。雖為泄劑，亦能益氣，故孫尚藥用治氣短不能接續，及大小七香丸、集香丸、諸湯散多用此也。又為肝經血分藥。

時珍曰：郁金入心，專治血分之病；薑黃入脾，兼治血中之氣；朮入肝，治氣中之血，稍為不同。按王執中《資生經》云：執中久患心脾疼，服醒脾藥反脹。用耆域所載蓬莪朮面裹炮熟研末，以水與酒醋煎服，立愈。蓋此藥能破氣中之血也。
results.translation:
**[Nomenclature]**
Common name: Péng é zhú [Zedoary; *Curcuma zedoaria*].

**[Collected Explanations]**
Zhi [Kou Zongshi or the *Kaibao* commentary source] states: Péng é zhú grows in the Western Rong [northwestern border] regions and in the various prefectures of Guangnan [southern Guangdong/Guangxi]. Its leaves resemble those of the lotus [*he*]; its seeds resemble dried mulberries; the rhizome [zhú] grows together beneath the root, one kind being good and another bad — the bad kind is toxic. The people of the Western Rong gather it by first letting sheep eat it; whatever the sheep refuse to eat is discarded.

Chen Cangqi [*Bencao Shiyi*] states: One variety is called péng é, which is black in color; a second is called [ILLEGIBLE: one character lost, likely a variant name], which is yellow; a third is called bō shā, which is sweet in flavor. Da Ming [Ri Huazi] states: This is the same as the root of jiāng huáng [turmeric, *Curcuma longa*] from the southern regions. That which grows in Hainan is called péng é zhú.

Su Song states: It is now found in places such as Jiangzhe [Jiangsu/Zhejiang]. Seedlings emerge in the third month, growing in fields and open land. The stalk is as wide as a coin and two to three feet tall. The leaves are bluish-white, one to two feet long and up to five cun broad, quite resembling lotus leaves. In the fifth month flowers appear in spikes, yellow in color with a slightly purple tip. The root resembles fresh ginger, with the rhizome [zhú] growing beneath the root, shaped like chicken or duck eggs.

**[Flavor and Nature]**
Bitter, acrid; warm; non-toxic.

Da Ming states: It is especially effective when used with wine or vinegar.

**[Indications and Treatments]**
Pain in the heart and abdomen; affliction by malign and pestilential [zhù wǔ] ghost-qi; cholera [huòluàn] with cold-qi; vomiting of sour water; detoxification; failure to digest food and drink — grind with wine and take orally. Also treats accumulations of blood and qi in women, and running piglet disorder [bēn tún] in men (*Kaibao bencao*). Breaks up concretions [pǐ] and cold-qi — grind with wine and vinegar and take orally (Zhen Quan). Treats all disorders of qi; opens the stomach and promotes digestion; unblocks menstruation; disperses blood stasis; stops pain and bleeding due to traumatic injury; and addresses internal injury with stagnant blood (Da Ming). Unblocks accumulated blood in the liver channel (Wang Haogu).

**[Elaborations and Discussion]**
Su Song states: Péng é zhú does not appear in ancient formulas. Today's physicians regard it as the most important drug for treating accumulations [jī jù] and various qi disorders. It works especially well when used together with jīng sān léng [bur-reed rhizome, *Sparganium stoloniferum*], and it is also widely used in women's medicinals.

Wang Haogu states: Péng é is black in color; it breaks blood within qi, and when combined with qi-regulating drugs it releases various aromatic [properties]. Although it functions as a dispersing agent [xiè jì], it can also tonify qi. Hence Sun Shangao [court pharmacist Sun] used it to treat shortness of breath with inability to draw a continuous breath, and it is also frequently used in preparations such as the Greater and Lesser Seven-Fragrance Pills [Qī Xiāng Wán], the Assembled Fragrances Pill [Jí Xiāng Wán], and various decoctions and powders. It further serves as a blood-aspect drug for the liver channel.

Li Shizhen states: Yù jīn [turmeric, *Curcuma wenyujin*, or *C. aromatica*] enters the heart and treats diseases of the blood aspect exclusively; jiāng huáng [*Curcuma longa*] enters the spleen and concurrently treats qi within the blood; é zhú [zedoary] enters the liver and treats blood within the qi — each is slightly different. Citing Wang Zhizhong's *Zīshēng Jīng* [Classic of Nurturing Life]: Zhizhong had long suffered from heart and spleen pain; taking spleen-rousing drugs only made the bloating worse. He then used the method recorded by Qīyù — wrapping péng é zhú in flour, roasting it until cooked, grinding it to a powder, and decocting it with water, wine, and vinegar — and was cured immediately. This is because this drug is able to break blood that is lodged within qi.

entities_mentioned.name: 蓬莪朮 (péng é zhú)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Curcuma zedoaria (zedoary)
entities_mentioned.notes: The primary subject of this entry. Distinguished from jiāng huáng and yù jīn within the text itself.

entities_mentioned.name: 波殺 (bō shā)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly a variety of Curcuma zedoaria or related Curcuma species with sweet taste
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as a third variety with a sweet flavor; may be a transliteration of a non-Chinese regional name.

entities_mentioned.name: 薑黃 (jiāng huáng)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Curcuma longa (turmeric)
entities_mentioned.notes: Mentioned as related to or identified with the southern root form; also discussed in Li Shizhen's comparative note.

entities_mentioned.name: 郁金 (yù jīn)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Curcuma wenyujin or Curcuma aromatica (wild turmeric / aromatic turmeric)
entities_mentioned.notes: Cited by Li Shizhen for comparison; enters the heart and treats blood-aspect diseases.

entities_mentioned.name: 荊三棱 (jīng sān léng)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Sparganium stoloniferum (bur-reed rhizome)
entities_mentioned.notes: Mentioned as a companion drug frequently combined with péng é zhú.

entities_mentioned.name: 七香丸 (Qī Xiāng Wán)
entities_mentioned.notes: A classical compound formula (both greater and lesser versions); contains péng é zhú.

entities_mentioned.name: 集香丸 (Jí Xiāng Wán)
entities_mentioned.notes: Another compound formula mentioned as containing péng é zhú.

results.translator_notes:
1. The second variety name in the 藏器 passage ('二名___，黃色') is missing or garbled in the source OCR; one character (likely a variant transcription) is lost. Marked as ILLEGIBLE in the translation.
2. '朮' throughout is used to refer specifically to the rhizome/tuber part of the plant growing below the root mass; it has been rendered as 'rhizome [zhú]' where it refers to the plant organ, and left as part of the plant name elsewhere.
3. '奔豚' (bēn tún, 'running piglet disorder') is a classical medical syndrome characterized by a surging sensation rising from the abdomen to the chest/throat; retained in brackets.
4. The phrase '面裹炮熟' means to wrap in (wheat) flour dough and then roast over fire until cooked through — a standard炮 processing technique. Translated as 'wrapping in flour [dough], roasting until cooked.'
5. '耆域' (Qīyù) refers to a legendary or historical Buddhist physician figure; the reference is to a text attributed to or associated with him.

results.original_text:
【釋名】京三棱（《開寶》）、草三棱（《開寶》）、雞爪三棱（《開寶》）、黑三棱（《圖
經》）、石三棱。頌曰︰三棱，葉有三棱也。生荊楚地，故名荊三棱以著其地。《開寶本草》
作京者誤矣。又出草三棱條，云即雞爪三棱，生蜀地。二月、八月採之。其實一類，隨形命
名爾，故並見之。

【集解】藏器曰︰三棱總有三、四種。京三棱，黃色體重，狀若鯽魚而小。又有黑三棱，狀如烏梅而稍大，體輕有須，相連蔓延，作漆色，蜀人以織爲器，一名 者，是也。療體並同。

頌曰︰京三棱舊不著所出地土，今荊襄、江淮、濟南、河陝間皆有之。多生淺水旁及陂澤中。春生苗，高三四尺，葉似莎草，極長，又似茭蒲葉而有三棱。五六月抽莖，高四五尺，大如人指，有三棱如削成。莖端開花，大體皆如莎草而大，黃紫色。苗下即魁，初
生成塊如附子大，或有扁者。其旁有根橫貫，一根則連數魁，魁上亦出苗。其魁皆扁長，如小鯽魚，體重者，三棱也。其根末將盡一魁，未發苗，小圓如烏梅者，黑三棱也。又根之端
鉤曲如爪者，雞爪三棱也，皆皮黑肌白而至輕。或云︰不出苗只生細根者，謂之雞爪三棱。
又不生細根者，謂之黑三棱，大小不常，其色黑，去皮即白。三者本一種，但力有剛柔，各適其用。

  "original_text": "【釋名】京三棱（《開寶》）、草三棱（《開寶》）、雞爪三棱（《開寶》）、黑三棱（《圖經》）、石三棱。頌曰︰三棱，葉有三棱也。生荊楚地，故名荊三棱以著其地。《開寶本草》作京者誤矣。又出草三棱條，云即雞爪三棱，生蜀地。二月、八月採之。其實一類，隨形命名爾，故並見之。

【集解】藏器曰︰三棱總有三、四種。京三棱，黃色體重，狀若鯽魚而小。又有黑三棱，狀如烏梅而稍大，體輕有須，相連蔓延，作漆色，蜀人以織爲器，一名者，是也。療體並同。

頌曰︰京三棱舊不著所出地土，今荊襄、江淮、濟南、河陝間皆有之。多生淺水旁及陂澤中。春生苗，高三四尺，葉似莎草，極長，又似茭蒲葉而有三棱。五六月抽莖，高四五尺，大如人指，有三棱如削成。莖端開花，大體皆如莎草而大，黃紫色。苗下即魁，初生成塊如附子大，或有扁者。其旁有根橫貫，一根則連數魁，魁上亦出苗。其魁皆扁長，如小鯽魚，體重者，三棱也。其根末將盡一魁，未發苗，小圓如烏梅者，黑三棱也。又根之端鉤曲如爪者，雞爪三棱也，皆皮黑肌白而至輕。或云︰不出苗只生細根者，謂之雞爪三棱。又不生細根者，謂之黑三棱，大小不常，其色黑，去皮即白。三者本一種，但力有剛柔，各適其用。因其形爲名，如烏頭、烏喙，雲母、雲華之類，本非兩物也。今人乃妄以鳧茈、香附子爲之。又河中府有石三棱，根黃白色，形如釵股，葉綠如蒲，苗高及尺，葉上亦有三棱，四月開花，白色如蓼花，五月採根，亦消積氣。今舉世所用三棱，皆淮南紅蒲根也，泰州尤多。其體至堅重，刻削魚形，葉扁莖圓，不復有三棱，不知何緣命名爲三棱也？雖太醫亦不以爲謬。流習既久，用根者不識其苗，采藥者莫究其用，因緣差失，不復辨別。今三棱皆獨旁引二根，無直下根，其形大體多如鯽魚。

時珍曰︰三棱多生荒廢陂池濕地。春時叢生

【氣味】苦，平，無毒。志曰︰甘，平，溫。大明曰︰甘、澀，涼。

元素曰︰苦、甘，無毒，陰中之陽。能瀉真氣。真氣虛者勿用。

【主治】老癖症瘕，積聚結塊，產後惡血血結，通月水，墮胎，止痛利氣（《開寶》）。治氣脹，破積氣，消撲損瘀血，婦人血脈不調，心腹痛，產後腹痛血運（大明）。心膈痛，飲食不消（元素）。通肝經積血，治瘡腫堅硬。

【發明】好古曰︰三棱色白屬金，破血中之氣，肝經血分藥也。三棱、莪朮治積塊瘡硬者，乃堅者削之也。

志曰︰俗傳昔人患症癖死，遺言令開腹取之。得病塊，乾硬如石，紋理有五色。以爲異物，削成刀柄。後因以刀刈三棱，柄消成水，乃知此藥可療症。

時珍曰︰三棱能破氣散結，故能治諸病。其功可近於香附而力峻，故難久服。按戴原禮《証治要訣》云︰有人病症癖腹脹，用三棱、莪朮，以酒煨煎服之，下一黑物如魚而愈也。",
  "translation": "**[Nomenclature]** Also known as: Jing Sanleng [Capital Three-edge] (Kaibao [Bencao]); Cao Sanleng [Grass Three-edge] (Kaibao); Jizhua Sanleng [Chicken-claw Three-edge] (Kaibao); Hei Sanleng [Black Three-edge] (Tujing [Bencao]); Shi Sanleng [Stone Three-edge].

Su Song states: "Sanleng" [Three-edge] refers to the three-edged leaves. The plant grows in the Jing-Chu [Hubei] region, hence the name Jing Sanleng to indicate its place of origin. The Kaibao Bencao's rendering of the character as "Jing" [capital] is erroneous. A separate entry for Cao Sanleng in that text identifies it as Jizhua Sanleng, said to grow in Shu [Sichuan], collected in the second and eighth months. In fact these are all of one kind, named acco

【釋名】雀頭香（《唐本》）、草附子（《圖經》）、水香棱（《圖經》）、水巴戟（《圖經》）、水莎（《圖經》）、侯莎（《爾雅》）、莎結（《圖經》）、夫須（《別錄》）、續根草（《圖經》）、地

時珍曰︰《別錄》止云莎草，不言用苗用根。後世皆用其根，名香附子，而不知莎草之名也。其草可爲笠及雨衣，疏而不沾，故字從草從沙。亦作蓑字，因其爲衣垂 ，如孝子衰衣之狀，故又從苔乃笠名，賤夫所須也。其根相附連續而生，可以合香，故謂之香附子。上古謂之雀頭香。
按《江表傳》云︰魏文帝遣使於吳求雀頭香，即此。其葉似三棱及巴戟，而生下濕地，故有水三棱、水巴戟之名。俗人呼爲雷公頭。《金光明經》謂之月萃哆。《記事珠》謂之抱靈居士。

【集解】《別錄》曰︰莎草生田野，二月、八月採。

弘景曰︰方藥不復用，古人爲詩多用之，而無識者。乃有鼠蓑，療體異此。

恭曰︰此草根名香附子，一名雀頭香，所在有之，莖葉都似三棱，合和香用之。

results.translator_notes:
{
  "title_inferred": "Suogen and Xiangfuzi (Cyperus rotundus / Nutgrass): Names, Origins, Properties, and Medical Uses",
  "original_text": "【釋名】雀頭香（《唐本》）、草附子（《圖經》）、水香棱（《圖經》）、水巴戟（《圖經》）、水莎（《圖經》）、侯莎（《爾雅》）、莎結（《圖經》）、夫須（《別錄》）、續根草（《圖經》）、地

時珍曰︰《別錄》止云莎草，不言用苗用根。後世皆用其根，名香附子，而不知莎草之名也。其草可爲笠及雨衣，疏而不沾，故字從草從沙。亦作蓑字，因其爲衣垂，如孝子衰衣之狀，故又從苔乃笠名，賤夫所須也。其根相附連續而生，可以合香，故謂之香附子。上古謂之雀頭香。
按《江表傳》云︰魏文帝遣使於吳求雀頭香，即此。其葉似三棱及巴戟，而生下濕地，故有水三棱、水巴戟之名。俗人呼爲雷公頭。《金光明經》謂之月萃哆。《記事珠》謂之抱靈居士。

【集解】《別錄》曰︰莎草生田野，二月、八月採。

弘景曰︰方藥不復用，古人爲詩多用之，而無識者。乃有鼠蓑，療體異此。

恭曰︰此草根名香附子，一名雀頭香，所在有之，莖葉都似三棱，合和香用之。

頌曰︰今處處有之。苗葉如薤而瘦，根如箸頭大。謹按︰唐玄宗《天寶單方圖》，載水香棱功狀與此相類。云水香棱原生博平郡池澤中，苗名香棱，根名莎結，亦名草附子。河南及淮南下濕地即有，名水莎。隴西謂之地根。蜀郡名續根草，亦名水巴戟。今涪都最饒，名三棱草。用莖作鞋履，所在皆有。采苗及花與根療病。

宗奭曰︰香附子今人多用。雖生於莎草根，然根上或有或無。有薄皸皮，紫黑色，非多毛也。刮去皮則色白。若便以根爲之，則誤矣。

時珍曰︰莎葉如老韭葉而硬，光澤有劍脊棱。五、六月中抽一莖，三棱中空，莖端復出數葉。開青花成穗如黍，中有細子。其根有須，須下結子一、二枚，轉相延生，子上有細黑毛，大者如羊棗而兩頭尖。采得燎去毛，曝乾貨之。此乃近時日用要藥，而陶氏不識，諸注亦略，乃知古今藥物興廢不同。如此則本草諸藥，亦不可以今之不識，便廢棄不收，安知異時不爲要藥如香附者乎？

根

【氣味】甘，微寒，無毒。宗奭曰︰苦。頌曰︰《天寶單方》云︰辛，微寒，無毒，性澀。

元素曰︰甘、苦，微寒，氣濃於味，陽中之陰，血中之氣藥也。

時珍曰︰辛、微苦、甘，平。足厥陰、手少陽藥也。能兼行十二經，入脈氣分。得童子小便、醋、芎藭、蒼朮良。

【主治】除胸中熱，充皮毛，久服利人，益氣，長須眉（《別錄》）。治心中客熱，膀胱間連脅下氣妨。常日憂愁不樂，兼心忪者（蘇頌）。治一切氣，霍亂吐瀉腹痛，腎氣膀胱冷氣（李杲）。散時氣寒疫，利三焦，解六郁，消飲食積聚，痰飲痞滿，腫腹脹，腳氣，止心腹肢體頭目齒耳諸痛，癰疽瘡瘍，吐血下血尿血，婦人崩漏帶下，月候不調，胎前產後百病（時珍）。

苗及花
丈夫心肺中虛風及客熱，膀胱間連脅下時有氣妨，皮膚瘙癢癮疹，飲食不多，日漸瘦損，常有憂愁心忪少氣等証。並收苗花二十餘斤銼細，以水二石五斗，煮一石五斗，斛中浸浴，令汗出五、六度，其瘙癢即止。四時常用，癮疹風永除（《天寶單方圖》）。煎飲散氣郁，利胸膈，降痰熱（時珍）。

【發明】好古曰︰香附治膀胱兩脅氣妨，心忪少氣，是能益氣，乃血中之氣藥也。本草不言治崩漏，而方中用治崩漏，是能益氣而止血也。又能逐去瘀血，是推陳也。正如巴豆治大便不通而又止泄瀉同意。又云︰香附陽中之陰，血中之氣藥，凡氣郁血氣必用之。炒黑能止血治崩漏，此婦人之仙藥也。多服亦能走氣。

震亨曰︰香附須用童子小便浸過，能總解諸郁，凡血氣必用之藥，引至氣分而生血，此正陰生陽長之義。本草不言補，而方家言於老人有益，意有存焉。蓋於行中有補理。天之所以爲天者，健而有常也。健運不息，所以生生無窮，即此理爾。今即香中亦用之。

時珍曰︰香附之氣平而不寒，香而能竄。其味多辛能散，微苦能降，微甘能和。乃足厥陰肝、手少陽三焦氣分主藥，而兼通十二經氣分。生則上行胸膈，外達皮膚；熟則下走肝腎，外徹腰足。炒黑則止血，得童溲浸炒則入血分而補虛，鹽水浸炒則入血分而潤燥，青鹽炒則補腎氣，酒浸炒則行經絡，醋浸炒則消積聚，薑汁炒則化痰飲。",

results.title_inferred: Ruixiang (Daphne / Chinese Spurge Laurel): Description, Root, and Medical Uses
results.original_text:
【集解】時珍曰︰南方州郡山中有之。枝幹婆娑，柔條濃葉，四時青茂。冬春之交，開花成簇，長三四分，如丁香狀，有黃、白、紫三色。《格古論》云︰瑞香高者三四尺，有數種︰有枇杷葉者，楊梅葉者，柯葉者，球子者，攣枝者。惟攣枝者花紫香烈，枇杷葉者結子。其始出於廬山，宋時人家栽之，始著名。攣枝者其節攣曲，如斷折之狀也。其根綿軟而香。

根

【氣味】甘、鹹，無毒。

【主治】急喉風，用白花者研水灌之（時珍。出《醫學集成》）
results.translation:
**[Collected Explanations]** Li Shizhen states: This plant is found in the mountains of southern prefectures and districts. Its branches and trunk spread gracefully; its slender branches bear thick leaves, and it remains green and flourishing throughout all four seasons. At the turn of winter and spring it blooms in clusters, the flowers three to four fen [approx. 1 cm] in length, shaped like clove [*dīng xiāng*] flowers, and occurring in three colors: yellow, white, and purple. The *Gegu Lun* ["Discourse on Investigating Antiquities"] states: Ruixiang can grow three to four chi [approx. 1–1.3 m] tall and comes in several varieties: those with loquat-shaped leaves, those with Chinese bayberry-shaped leaves, those with oak-like leaves [*kē* leaves], those that bear round fruits, and those with contorted branches. Of these, only the contorted-branch variety has intensely fragrant purple flowers, while the loquat-leaf variety produces seeds. The plant originally came from Mount Lu, and was cultivated in private gardens during the Song dynasty, when it first became famous. The contorted-branch variety has nodes that are bent and twisted, as if the branches had been broken and rejoined. The root is soft and fibrous, and fragrant.

**Root**

**[Qi and Flavor]** Sweet and salty; non-toxic.

**[Indications and Treatments]** Acute throat wind [*jí hóu fēng*; sudden severe throat obstruction/infection]: grind the white-flowered variety [of the root] with water and administer by pouring into the throat. (Li Shizhen; sourced from the *Yixue Jicheng* ["Compendium of Medical Learning"])

entities_mentioned.name: 瑞香 (Ruixiang)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Daphne odora (Winter Daphne / Rose Daphne)
entities_mentioned.notes: A fragrant ornamental shrub in the family Thymelaeaceae, native to China. The description of clustered flowers in winter-spring, the soft fragrant root, and the multiple leaf-type varieties all align well with Daphne odora and related Daphne species.

entities_mentioned.name: 丁香 (Dīng xiāng)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Syzygium aromaticum (Clove)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used here as a shape comparison for the flowers of Ruixiang, not as a medicinal ingredient.

entities_mentioned.name: 急喉風 (jí hóu fēng)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Acute throat obstruction / severe acute pharyngolaryngeal infection or epiglottitis
entities_mentioned.notes: A traditional Chinese medical term for a sudden, severe condition of the throat involving swelling, obstruction, and difficulty breathing or swallowing.

entities_mentioned.name: 《格古論》(Gegu Lun)
entities_mentioned.notes: Likely a reference to 《格古要論》(Gegu Yaolun, 'Essential Criteria for Evaluating Antiquities') by Cao Zhao (c. 1388, Ming dynasty), a well-known connoisseurship text.

entities_mentioned.name: 《醫學集成》(Yixue Jicheng)
entities_mentioned.notes: A Ming-dynasty medical compendium cited as the source for the throat treatment. The exact edition or author is unclear.

results.translator_notes: The preparation method for acute throat wind specifies using the white-flowered variety (白花者) of Ruixiang root, ground with water and poured into the throat — a form of emergency oral administration. The measurement 'three to four fen' (三四分) refers to traditional Chinese units where 1 fen ≈ 3.2 mm, so the flowers are roughly 1–1.3 cm long. 'Acute throat wind' (急喉風) was a broad category encompassing various life-threatening throat emergencies in classical Chinese medicine. The mention of 攣枝 (contorted/knotted branches) as the most fragrant variety corresponds to cultivars of Daphne odora prized in Chinese horticulture. No OCR errors of significance were detected in the source text.
